13.27: “You put my feet in stocks, you keep watch over all my paths, you mark the very prints of my feet.” The surveillance and restraint Job describes are the conditions of the condemned under divine judicial wrath. Christ entered precisely these conditions: arrested, bound (John 18:12), and executed under the gaze of the divine judgment he was bearing. Yet his feet were marked — the prints of the nails became the marks of identification and verification in his resurrection appearances (John 20:25,27; Luke 24:40).
13.28: “And I waste away like something rotten, like a garment eaten through by moths.” The wasting of the human body under sustained suffering is the condition of Isa 53:2–3: “he had no beauty or majesty to attract us to him, nothing in his appearance that we should desire him; he was despised and rejected by mankind, a man of suffering.” Christ bore the full degradation of human wasting, not preserved from it: “by his wounds we are healed” (Isa 53:5; 1 Pet 2:24).

14.1: A direct revelation: “man born of woman is short-lived and full of turmoil.” Paul uses this exact phrase — “born of woman” (genomenon ek gynaikos) — for Christ’s incarnation in Gal 4:4–5: “God sent his Son, born of woman, born under the law, to redeem those under the law.” Christ is the “man born of woman” who, being short-lived and full of turmoil in his humanity, redeems precisely the human condition Job describes — not by escaping it but by entering and bearing it to its depths.
14.2: Man comes up like a flower that wilts and passes like a shadow that does not endure — the poetic statement of human transience that Christ entered fully. Heb 2:14–15: “since the children have flesh and blood, he too shared in their humanity so that by his death he might break the power of him who holds the power of death.” Christ did not bypass the withering and the shadow-passing; he went through it — and by going through it he reversed the outcome, flowering again in resurrection (1 Cor 15:20).
14.3: “Yet on such a creature do you fix your eyes, and bring him into court before you?” Job’s amazement that God fixes his eyes on frail humanity echoes Ps 8:4: “what is man that you are mindful of him, the son of man that you care for him?” Heb 2:6–9 applies Ps 8 to Christ, who “for a little while was made lower than the angels” specifically to identify with this fragile creature — but “now crowned with glory and honor because he suffered death.” God’s fixed gaze on frail humanity finds its fullest expression in the incarnation.
14.4: “Who can bring something pure from something impure? No one can.” This impossibility statement is the theological problem that Christ’s virgin birth and sinless humanity address. He was “born of woman” (v1; Gal 4:4) yet was the pure from the impure lineage: “without sin” (Heb 4:15; 1 Pet 2:22). The one born of sinful human stock who was yet without sin is the necessary condition of the incarnation’s redemptive purpose — only the pure can make the impure pure (1 John 1:7).
14.5: “Since his days are fixed, the number of his months determined by you, the limit set that he cannot cross.” Christ came “in the fullness of time” (Gal 4:4) — the divinely fixed moment. The “hour” of Christ’s death was specifically predetermined: “no one could seize him, because his hour had not yet come” (John 7:30; 8:20); “now the Son of Man is glorified” (John 13:31 when the hour arrives). The fixed number of his months was precisely three decades of earthly life before the appointed hour of his death.
14.6: “Look away from him so he can rest, until he has completed his time like a hired worker.” Christ is the one who calls the weary to rest: “come to me, all you who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest” (Matt 11:28–30). He himself rested in the tomb on the Sabbath after completing the work of redemption — “it is finished” (John 19:30; Heb 4:10: “anyone who enters God’s rest also rests from their works, just as God did from his”). The completion of the hired servant’s time anticipates the completed work.
14.7: A type: “there is hope for a tree: if it is cut down, it will sprout again, and its new growth will not stop.” This tree-hope is the structural type underlying Isa 11:1: “a shoot will come up from the stump of Jesse; from his roots a Branch will bear fruit.” The Davidic dynasty was cut down in exile; Christ is the shoot from the stump. The cross appears to cut down the tree finally; the resurrection is the sprout from the cut stump. 1 Cor 15:37: “what you sow does not come to life unless it dies” — the grain of wheat must fall into the ground (John 12:24).
14.8: “Though its root grows old in the earth and its stump dies in the soil.” The dying of the root in the earth is the death and burial stage of the type: the Tree of Jesse apparently extinguished, the Davidic line apparently ended. The three days in the tomb correspond to the seed in the ground: “unless a kernel of wheat falls to the ground and dies, it remains only a single seed; but if it dies, it produces many seeds” (John 12:24). Death is not the end of the tree — it is the necessary precondition of its renewal.
14.9: A direct type: “yet at the smell of water it will bud and put out branches like a new plant.” The resurrection of the cut tree at the touch of water is the resurrection type that Job 14 offers the OT. The resurrection of Christ is the fulfillment: “Christ has indeed been raised from the dead, the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep” (1 Cor 15:20). The “new plant” that sprouts from the old stump is the new creation that emerges from Christ’s death — “if anyone is in Christ, the new creation has come” (2 Cor 5:17).
14.10: “But man dies and lies helpless; he breathes his last — and where is he?” This is the theological problem that the entire NT answers. Christ’s resurrection is the definitive response to “where is he?” — the empty tomb is the answer: he is not here, he is risen (Luke 24:6). John 11:25: “I am the resurrection and the life; the one who believes in me will live, even though they die.” The “where is he?” of death becomes the “he is here” of the resurrection appearances (John 20:19–29).
14.11: As water vanishes from a lake and a river wastes away and dries up — so Job describes the irreversible disappearance of the dead. Christ reversed irreversibility: “I am the resurrection and the life; whoever lives by believing in me will never die” (John 11:26). The water that vanishes from the lake is the same element Christ uses to describe eternal life: “a spring of water welling up to eternal life” (John 4:14) — the water that does not vanish but rises eternally.
14.12: “So man lies down and does not rise; until the heavens are no more, they will not wake or be roused from their sleep.” Job’s “until the heavens are no more” is the eschatological limit: the moment when the heavens pass away. Rev 21:1: “I saw a new heaven and a new earth, for the first heaven and the first earth had passed away.” Christ is the one who brings this “until” to pass and raises the dead in connection with it (1 Thess 4:16: “the dead in Christ will rise first”; 1 Cor 15:23–26).
14.13: “O that you would hide me in Sheol until your anger passes, set a fixed time for me, and then remember me.” Job’s longing to be hidden in Sheol and then remembered is the deepest OT prayer for post-death divine remembrance. Christ entered Sheol — “you will not abandon me to the realm of the dead” (Acts 2:27, quoting Ps 16:10); “he was put to death in the body but made alive in the Spirit” (1 Pet 3:18). He was remembered by the Father and raised — the enacted answer to Job’s desperate wish.
14.14: A direct revelation: “if a man dies, will he live again? Through all my days of hard service I will wait until my release comes.” This is the central question of the book of Job and of all human existence. Christ’s resurrection is the definitive answer. 1 Cor 15:12–22: “if Christ has not been raised, your faith is futile” — and since Christ has been raised, the answer to Job’s “will he live again?” is an unreserved yes. The “release” Job waits for is the resurrection release that Christ obtained first and now shares (Heb 2:14–15).
14.15: A direct revelation: “you will call out to me, and I will answer; you will long for the work of your hands.” The divine longing for the creature — “you will long for the work of your hands” — is one of the most tender statements of divine love in Job. It finds its ultimate expression in the resurrection: Jesus calling Mary’s name (“Mary!” John 20:16) and her turning to him is the enacted fulfillment — the Creator calling, the creature answering. The resurrection of the dead is God longing for and reclaiming the work of his hands.
14.16: “But now you count my every step and watch over my sin without fail.” The divine surveillance that condemns is what Christ bore for us: “the LORD has laid on him the iniquity of us all” (Isa 53:6). His steps were counted, his path was watched — and declared perfectly righteous. For those in Christ, the divine surveillance no longer condemns because he was “delivered over to death for our sins and was raised to life for our justification” (Rom 4:25). The count of every step now yields acquittal, not condemnation.
14.17: “My transgression is sealed in a bag; you have stitched up my guilt.” The sin sealed in a bag is the image of the permanent record held against the accused. Christ unsealed and emptied that bag: “he forgave us all our sins, having canceled the charge of our legal indebtedness, which stood against us and condemned us; he has taken it away, nailing it to the cross” (Col 2:13–14). The bag that was sealed is now empty; the stitched guilt is now torn open and forgiven.
14.18: A mountain eroding comes to nothing; a rock dislodged from its place — all human stability eventually crumbles. Christ is the Rock that does not crumble: 1 Cor 10:4 (“they drank from the spiritual rock that accompanied them, and that rock was Christ”); Matt 16:18 (“on this rock I will build my church, and the gates of Hades will not overcome it”). The erosion that Job describes as characteristic of all earthly things is precisely what Christ does not undergo: “he who is built on this rock will not fall” (Matt 7:25).
14.19: “Water wears down the stones; floods wash away the soil of the earth — and so you wash away the hope of man.” Job’s conclusion that all human hope is eroded by the waters of death and time is the verdict that Christ’s resurrection overturns. He is “the hope of glory” (Col 1:27: “Christ in you, the hope of glory”). The hope of man is not washed away in Christ — rather, he provides an “anchor for the soul, firm and secure” (Heb 6:19–20) that no flood of suffering or death can erode.
14.20: “You overpower him completely and he is gone; you alter his countenance and send him away.” Death’s overpowering is the condition Christ entered and reversed. He was overpowered by death (Mark 15:37: “and with a loud cry, Jesus breathed his last”); his countenance was altered beyond recognition (Isa 52:14: “his appearance was so disfigured beyond that of any human being”). But then: “I am the Living One; I was dead, and now look, I am alive for ever and ever! And I hold the keys of death and Hades” (Rev 1:18). He was overpowered and he overpowered death.
14.21: “His sons rise to honor but he does not know it; they are brought low but he is not aware.” Job describes the isolation of the dead from their descendants. Christ’s resurrection broke this isolation: as the “firstborn among many brothers” (Rom 8:29) he lives and participates in the honor of his brothers and sisters who believe. Heb 2:11–12: “he is not ashamed to call them brothers and sisters” — the risen Christ is aware of and invested in the honor and lowliness of those who are his, unlike the dead in Sheol who know nothing.
14.22: “He feels only the pain within him and mourns only over himself.” The dead man’s self-absorbed pain is the final picture of death’s isolation — the ultimate loneliness. Christ’s death was the radical opposite: even on the cross he turned outward (“Father, forgive them,” Luke 23:34; “today you will be with me in paradise,” Luke 23:43; caring for his mother, John 19:26–27). His resurrection ends the isolation of death and opens the community of the new creation. The self-mourning of Job 14 is the condition Christ entered; the self-giving of the cross is the condition he enacted; the community of the resurrection is the condition he opens.

15.1: Eliphaz’s second speech intensifies the accusation: he begins by attacking Job’s speech itself as wind (v2), refusing to fear God (v4), and iniquity out of his own mouth (v5). In the NT, those who attack the righteous sufferer’s speech are typed in Christ’s accusers: the high priest’s question (“have you no answer to make?”, Mark 14:60), the mockers at the cross who used Jesus’s own words against him (Matt 27:42–43). Eliphaz functions as the wisdom-tradition accuser whose confident theology has become a weapon against the innocent.
15.2: Eliphaz accuses Job of answering with windy knowledge (de’ah ruah) and filling his belly with east wind. In the NT, the opponents of the suffering Christ likewise charged that his words were without authority (John 7:15: “how does this man know letters, having never studied?”). The charge that suffering produces invalid theology is the perpetual accusation against those whose theology is shaped by genuine encounter with divine mystery rather than received tradition.
15.3: The question whether a righteous man should argue with unprofitable talk uses the wisdom tradition’s contempt for speech that produces nothing constructive. In contrast, Christ’s speech — even in its most suffering form — is productive: the seven last words from the cross are among the most theologically dense utterances in the NT. The tradition that dismisses suffering-speech as “unprofitable talk” is the tradition that failed to hear the divine voice in the cross.
15.4: Eliphaz’s charge that Job is undermining the fear of God (tafeer yir’ah) and hindering meditation before God inverts the reality: Job’s speech is a model of genuine encounter with God through suffering. In the NT, the high priests similarly accused Jesus of undermining religious devotion (John 11:48: “if we let him go on like this, everyone will believe in him”). The charge of undermining religion is regularly leveled against those who, like Job, challenge the comfortable theology that protects institutions from divine judgment.
15.5: Eliphaz accuses Job’s iniquity of teaching his mouth and his choosing the tongue of the crafty. In the NT, the scribes and Pharisees charged Jesus with speaking by the power of Beelzebul (Matt 12:24) — attributing the genuine divine speech to demonic origin. The pattern: the accuser who cannot recognize God speaking through suffering must attribute the speech to wickedness or deception. The innocent sufferer’s speech is always misattributed by those whose theology has no category for innocent suffering.
15.6: Eliphaz says that Job’s own mouth condemns him, not Eliphaz. This anticipates the Pharisees’ attempt to catch Jesus in his own words (Luke 20:20: “spies who pretended to be sincere, that they might catch him in something he said”; Matt 22:15: “snare him in his talk”). The tactic of using the sufferer’s own words as evidence of guilt is the oldest prosecutorial strategy in the book of Job and a recurring feature of Christ’s trial.
15.7: The rhetorical question — were you the first man born, were you brought forth before the hills? — challenges Job’s wisdom with a creation-priority argument. The NT answers this exact question about Christ: “He is the image of the invisible God, the firstborn of all creation” (Col 1:15); “before Abraham was, I am” (John 8:58). The very question that Eliphaz uses to dismiss Job’s wisdom is the question that Christ answers affirmatively: yes, the pre-existent Logos was there before the hills.
15.8: The divine council question (“have you listened in the council of God and do you limit wisdom to yourself?”) ironically describes the very access that Christ, the eternal Son, has: Prov 8:22–31 describes Wisdom present at creation and daily beside the Creator. The prologue of Job (1:6–12) shows that the real divine council has already decided Job’s case. Eliphaz’s accusation that Job claims divine-council access is simultaneously correct about Christ (who has full access) and wrong about Job (who is nonetheless right about his own innocence).
15.9: Eliphaz’s “what do you know that we do not know?” dismisses Job’s experiential knowledge. In the NT, the Pharisees’ dismissal of Jesus’ teaching with “who gave you this authority?” (Matt 21:23) is the identical challenge. The wisdom that suffering produces — the knowledge of God’s faithfulness in darkness, the experiential theology of the cross — is precisely the knowledge that the comfortable wisdom tradition lacks.
15.10: The grey-haired and aged on Eliphaz’s side, older than Job’s father, represent the appeal to tradition as the final authority. Christ similarly faced the appeal to Mosaic tradition (John 9:28: “we are disciples of Moses”). The divine wisdom that speaks through suffering is consistently newer than the tradition that opposes it, yet it expresses what the tradition was always pointing toward without recognizing it.
15.11: Eliphaz offers the “consolations of God” (tanhumot ’el) as a gentle word. In 2 Cor 1:3–5, Paul describes the God who is “the Father of mercies and God of all comfort, who comforts us in all our affliction.” The true consolations of God come not through the friends’ theological lectures but through participation in the suffering of Christ (2 Cor 1:5: “as we share abundantly in Christ’s sufferings, so through Christ we share abundantly in comfort too”). Eliphaz offers consolation without participation; Christ offers consolation through participation.
15.12: The question “why does your heart carry you away, and why do your eyes flash?” accuses Job of emotional instability underlying his theology. The NT shows Jesus weeping at Lazarus’s tomb (John 11:35) and experiencing deep distress in Gethsemane (Matt 26:37: “he began to be sorrowful and troubled”). The emotion of the sufferer before the mystery of death and divine hiddenness is not instability but depth of engagement with reality; Eliphaz’s charge of emotional instability is the accusation of those who have never faced what Job and Jesus faced.
15.13: Eliphaz’s charge that Job turns his spirit against God and lets such words go out of his mouth anticipates the passion narrative accusation of blasphemy. Mark 14:64: the high priest says “you have heard his blasphemy” after Jesus’s claim to be seated at the right hand of Power. The religious establishment’s category for the sufferer’s honest speech to God is blasphemy; the NT will show that what appeared to be blasphemy was in fact the Son speaking truthfully about himself.
15.14: “What is man that he can be pure, or he who is born of a woman that he can be righteous?” — this rhetorical question is the theological heart of the anthropological problem that the incarnation answers. The incarnate Christ, “born of a woman, born under the law” (Gal 4:4), is the one born of woman who is fully righteous. Matt 11:11 uses the identical phrase “born of women” (“among those born of women there has arisen no one greater than John the Baptist”) precisely to mark the category that Christ transcends. Eliphaz’s rhetorical question has a real answer: the one born of a virgin who is simultaneously fully God.
15.15: “Behold, God puts no trust even in his holy ones, and the heavens are not pure in his sight” — this theology of universal impurity before God is the backdrop against which Christ’s unique purity stands out. Heb 7:26–27: “it was fitting that we should have a high priest, holy, innocent, unstained, separated from sinners, exalted above the heavens. He has no need, like those high priests, to offer sacrifices daily, first for his own sins and then for those of the people.” Christ is the exception to Eliphaz’s universalized impurity: the one mortal born of woman who is truly pure.
15.16: “How much less one who is abominable and corrupt, a man who drinks injustice like water” — the double descent of v15–16 (even the holy ones/heavens are impure, so how much less corrupt humanity) uses the same logic as the divine speeches (Job 40:19: the creature is dwarfed by the Creator). The NT applies this argument in reverse for Christ: if even holy ones are impure before God, and Christ is purer than all — then Christ is the singular exception who surpasses the entire creaturely order.
15.17: Eliphaz’s offer to show Job wisdom he has seen uses the posture of the authoritative teacher passing down traditional wisdom. The Sermon on the Mount (Matt 5:21: “you have heard that it was said to those of old…but I say to you”) inverts this: Christ speaks not from tradition but from divine authority. The contrast between Eliphaz teaching from the tradition of the fathers and Christ teaching as the one who establishes what the fathers were pointing toward is the structural contrast between law-wisdom and incarnate Wisdom.
15.18: The wisdom that “wise men have told, and their fathers have not hidden” is the received tradition that Eliphaz appeals to. In the NT, Paul describes “the mystery hidden for ages and generations but now revealed to his saints” (Col 1:26) — the wisdom that was not fully visible in the tradition, hidden in types and figures, now disclosed in Christ. Eliphaz’s appeal to what has always been taught is precisely the category that cannot contain the new thing God is doing in the incarnation.
15.19: The appeal to those “to whom alone the land was given, and no stranger passed among them” is an ethnic purity argument: the pure wisdom lineage of the patriarchal age. The NT explicitly breaks this: the gospel is for all nations (Matt 28:19; Eph 3:6: “the Gentiles are fellow heirs, members of the same body”). The Christological revelation is precisely the revelation to the nations that the “land-restricted” ethnic wisdom could not have anticipated.
15.20: The wicked man writhing in pain all his days begins the long description of the wicked person’s misery (vv20–35). This description functions as the mirror-image of the Suffering Servant: the wicked person suffers for his own sin (Eliphaz’s claim about Job); the Servant suffers not for his own sin but for others (Isa 53:4–5). The verses describing the wicked are typologically significant not as direct prophecy but as structural contrast: every element of the wicked one’s misery that Eliphaz applies to Job will ultimately be borne by Christ on behalf of the truly wicked.
15.21: Terrifying sounds in the wicked man’s ears, a destroyer coming on him in prosperity anticipates the sudden arrest of Christ at Gethsemane: in the middle of a week of triumphal entry and teaching, the destroyer came suddenly. Luke 22:53: “this is your hour and the power of darkness.” The terror that the wicked man carries through his days is the terror that Christ voluntarily entered in Gethsemane, knowing what was coming.
15.22: The wicked man not believing he will return from darkness, destined for the sword anticipates Christ’s voluntary entry into death: “he humbled himself by becoming obedient to the point of death, even death on a cross” (Phil 2:8). Unlike the wicked man who cannot escape the sword, Christ freely submitted to it. The darkness he did not believe he would escape is the darkness Christ entered and conquered (John 1:5: “the light shines in the darkness, and the darkness has not overcome it”).
15.23: The wicked wandering for bread, knowing the day of darkness is ready at hand, resonates with the one who voluntarily became poor (2 Cor 8:9: “though he was rich, yet for your sake he became poor, so that you by his poverty might become rich”). Christ identified with the wandering poor; the day of darkness — the crucifixion — was the day he had been moving toward since Bethlehem.
15.24: Distress and anguish terrifying the wicked like a king ready to attack uses military language that Gethsemane embodies: “my soul is very sorrowful, even to death” (Matt 26:38). Christ entered the distress and anguish of facing the full weight of divine judgment — not because he was wicked, but because he bore the wicked’s penalty. The anguish that the wicked deserve for their own sin, Christ experienced on their behalf.
15.25: The wicked man stretching out his hand against God, running defiantly against the Almighty, is the picture of human rebellion that reaches its climax in the crucifixion: the chief priests and crowd who cried “crucify him” were in this deepest sense stretching their hands against God incarnate. Acts 4:26–28 applies Ps 2:2 (“the kings of the earth set themselves against the LORD”) to the events of the crucifixion: the gathering of Herod, Pontius Pilate, the Gentiles, and Israel against God’s anointed was the supreme instance of running against the Almighty.
15.26: Running against God with a thick-bossed shield describes aggressive defiance of the divine. At Calvary, the mockers under the cross “derided him, wagging their heads” (Mark 15:29) — the ancient gesture of aggression and contempt. The human boldness before the apparently defeated Christ on the cross was the ultimate expression of the wicked man’s deluded confidence in his own strength against God.
15.27: The wicked man with a fat face, gathering fat on his waist, describes physical prosperity fueling spiritual complacency. Luke 12:19–20 (the rich fool with barns full of goods): “Soul, you have ample goods laid up for many years; relax, eat, drink, be merry’—But God said to him, ‘Fool! This night your soul is required of you.’” The fat-gathered prosperity becomes the occasion of sudden divine demand — the same pattern Eliphaz describes.
15.28: The wicked living in desolate cities, in houses no one should inhabit, ready to become heaps of rubble anticipates the “desolation” language of the eschatological discourse (Matt 24:15: “the abomination of desolation”; Luke 21:20: “when you see Jerusalem surrounded by armies, then know that its desolation has come near”). The ruins that the wicked inhabit are the ultimate expression of the city that rejects God’s messenger (Matt 23:38: “your house is left to you desolate”).
15.29: The wicked not growing rich, with his wealth not enduring, not extending his possessions over the earth, anticipates the NT pattern of the rich who will lose everything: “do not lay up for yourselves treasures on earth” (Matt 6:19); “the cares of the world and the deceitfulness of riches choke the word” (Matt 13:22). Christ himself had nowhere to lay his head (Luke 9:58) as the deliberate embodiment of the non-accumulation that the wicked fail to practice by choice.
15.30: The wicked not escaping darkness, the flame withering his shoots, his blossom blown away by the wind of God’s mouth uses vegetation metaphors for destroyed potential. John 15:6: “whoever does not abide in me is thrown away like a branch and withers; and the branches are gathered, thrown into the fire, and burned.” The divine wind/breath that blows away the wicked man’s shoots is the same divine judgment that the final separation accomplishes — the withering of those who do not remain in Christ, the true Vine.
15.31: The warning not to trust in emptiness and be deceived (self-deception leading to worthlessness) anticipates Christ’s warning about false prophets (Matt 7:15–16: “beware of false prophets…you will recognize them by their fruits”) and false security (Matt 7:26: “everyone who hears these words of mine and does not do them will be like a foolish man who built his house on the sand”). The deception that produces emptiness is the anti-type of the truth that produces fruit (John 15:5).
15.32: The wicked man filled before his time, his branch not green, uses the premature-ending metaphor for a life that was directed toward false ends. Christ’s death, by contrast, appeared to be a premature ending but was actually the fulfillment of purpose: John 12:24: “unless a grain of wheat falls into the earth and dies, it remains alone; but if it dies, it bears much fruit.” The ending that looked like failure was the precondition for the fruit that now fills the world.
15.33: The wicked like a vine casting off unripe grapes, shaking off blossoms like an olive tree describes unsuccessful fruiting — the tree that looks productive but produces nothing edible. Mark 11:12–14 (Jesus cursing the fig tree with leaves but no fruit) applies this exact image to Israel’s religious establishment: the appearance of fruitfulness (leaves/blossoms) with no actual fruit. The cursed fig tree that withers is the NT’s anti-type to Christ as the true Vine and true fruit-producer.
15.34: The congregation of the godless being barren, fire consuming the tents of bribery, anticipates the judgment against the religious establishment that opposed Jesus: Matt 23:38 (“your house is left to you desolate”); Luke 19:43–44 (Jerusalem’s destruction). The fire that consumes the tents of those who corrupt justice is the judgment that Jerusalem’s rejection of the Son brought upon it in 70 CE.
15.35: Conceiving trouble and bringing forth evil, with their belly preparing deceit, describes the full cycle of sin from internal conception to external expression (cf. Jas 1:15: “sin when it is fully grown brings forth death”). The contrast with Christ is complete: he conceived no sin, brought forth no evil, and prepared no deceit (1 Pet 2:22: “he committed no sin, neither was deceit found in his mouth”; Isa 53:9). Job’s friends have applied to him the description that belongs only to those who are genuinely guilty; the one to whom the description truly does not apply is Christ.

16.1: Job’s designation of the friends as “miserable comforters” (menahame ’amal, comforters of trouble/evil) is the structural opposite of the Spirit who is the Paraclete (Comforter, John 14:16,26; 15:26; 16:7). The friends comfort with theology that adds to suffering; the Spirit comforts through the truth of Christ’s completed work. In 2 Cor 1:4, Paul describes God as one who “comforts us in all our affliction, so that we may be able to comfort those who are in any affliction.” The friends’ miserable comfort points by contrast to the genuine comfort that flows from participation in Christ’s own suffering and resurrection.
16.2: The “many such things I have heard” from the friends describes the repetitive nature of conventional theological consolation. In the passion narrative, the bystanders at the cross also repeated conventional religious formulas (“if you are the Son of God, come down from the cross,” Matt 27:40–42). The gap between the conventional formula and the actual experience of God-forsakenness is the gap that only Ps 22 (“my God, my God, why have you forsaken me?”) can bridge.
16.3: Job asks: “shall windy words have an end, or what provokes you that you answer?” — the challenge to empty theological speech. James 1:19: “let every person be quick to hear, slow to speak, slow to anger.” The capacity to listen to the suffering person without immediately defending God’s reputation is the practical wisdom that the friends entirely lack and that Jesus embodies at Lazarus’s tomb (John 11:33–35: he saw Mary weeping, “he was deeply moved in his spirit and greatly troubled” — then wept — before performing the miracle).
16.4: Job says he could make speeches against them and shake his head at them if their positions were reversed. The head-shaking of contempt (nud) is the gesture of the mockers at the crucifixion: Matt 27:39 (“those who passed by derided him, wagging their heads”) fulfills Ps 22:7 (“all who see me mock me; they make mouths at me; they shake their heads”). Job’s observation that the gesture of contempt is easy when it is not your suffering is the precondition for understanding why the mockery at Calvary is so theologically significant.
16.5: If Job were in their position, he would strengthen them with his mouth and the solace of his lips would assuage their grief. This describes the pastoral function that Christ’s own suffering makes uniquely possible: Heb 4:15 (“we do not have a high priest who is unable to sympathize with our weaknesses, but one who in every respect has been tempted as we are, yet without sin”). The one who has suffered as Job suffered can bring the solace that the comfortable friends cannot offer.
16.6: “If I speak, my pain is not assuaged; and if I forbear, how much of it leaves me?” describes the intractable nature of Job’s suffering regardless of speech or silence. In Gethsemane, Christ prayed three times the same prayer (Matt 26:39,42,44) — the repetition of prayer that does not change the cup is the prayer of the one whose suffering is real and not removed by spoken words. The suffering that does not respond to either speaking or silence is the suffering that goes to the cross.
16.7: “Surely now he has worn me out; you have made desolate all my company” — the divine exhaustion of Job and the desolation of his community anticipates the passion: “they all left him and fled” (Mark 14:50). The abandonment of the suffering one by his community is a structural feature of the Passion that Job anticipates: the sufferer is both worn out by God and left by those who should have stayed.
16.8: God seizing Job (“and you have shriveled me up, which is a witness against me; and my leanness has risen up against me”) describes the body bearing the marks of suffering as testimony against itself. The body of the crucified Christ bore the marks of suffering — and those marks, post-resurrection, became the evidence of his identity and the ground of faith (John 20:27: “put your finger here, and see my hands; and put out your hand, and place it in my side”). The marks that function as accusation in the friends’ theology become, in resurrection, the marks of the Savior’s identity.
16.9: “He has torn me in his wrath and hated me; he has gnashed his teeth at me” echoes Ps 22:13 (“they open wide their mouths at me, like a ravening and roaring lion”) and Lam 2:16 (“all your enemies open their mouths wide against you; they hiss and gnash their teeth”). The divine wrath that Job describes and the human wrath at the cross are convergent in Christ’s passion: Christ bore the wrath of God against sin (Rom 3:25: the propitiation) while simultaneously bearing the wrath of humans (Acts 2:23: “this Jesus, delivered up according to the definite plan and foreknowledge of God, you crucified and killed by the hands of lawless men”).
16.10: “They have gaped at me with their mouths; they have struck my cheeks with contempt; they mob together against me” — this is the most direct Passion parallel in Job 16. Isa 50:6: “I gave my back to those who strike, and my cheeks to those who pull out the beard; I hid not my face from disgrace and spitting.” Matt 26:67: “then they spit in his face and struck him. And some slapped him.” Luke 22:63: “the men who were holding Jesus mocked him and beat him.” Job’s suffering as the struck, mocked, surrounded sufferer is the pattern of the Passion.
16.11: God delivering Job to the ungodly and casting him into the hands of the wicked (“God gives me up to the ungodly and casts me into the hands of the wicked”) is the theological summary of the betrayal and trial of Jesus: Luke 24:7 (“the Son of Man must be delivered into the hands of sinful men and be crucified”); Acts 2:23 (“delivered up according to the definite plan and foreknowledge of God”). The divine delivering-up is not abandonment but the deliberate handing-over that the redemptive plan required.
16.12: “I was at ease and he broke me apart; he seized me by the neck and dashed me to pieces; he set me up as his target” — the shattered tranquility by divine seizure describes the arrest in Gethsemane. Jesus was at ease (“I have said these things to you, that in me you may have peace,” John 16:33) and then seized (John 18:12: “the band of soldiers and their captain and the officers of the Jews arrested Jesus and bound him”). Job as God’s target, set up for destruction, is the pattern of the Lamb whom God set forth as a propitiation (Rom 3:25).
16.13: God’s archers surrounding Job, splitting his kidneys without mercy, and pouring out his gall on the ground anticipates the spear thrust at the crucifixion (John 19:34: “one of the soldiers pierced his side with a spear, and at once blood and water came out”) and fulfills Ps 22:16 (“they have pierced my hands and feet”). The body surrounded and pierced by divine arrows is the typological image of the incarnate body surrounded by soldiers and pierced on the cross.
16.14: “He breaks me with breach upon breach; he runs at me like a warrior” describes the compounding of wounds. The crucifixion was a cumulative violence: scourging, crown of thorns, carrying the cross, nailing, the spear thrust — breach upon breach. The divine warrior who attacks is also, in the passion, the one who absorbs the divine warrior’s assault: at Calvary God’s own Son receives the breach-upon-breach that the sin of humanity deserved.
16.15: Sewing sackcloth on skin and thrusting his horn in the dust describes the complete loss of dignity and status: from the honored leader of 29:7–10 to the dust-horn sackcloth wearer. Christ’s kenosis (Phil 2:7–8: “emptied himself, taking the form of a servant…he humbled himself by becoming obedient to the point of death”) is the voluntary version of this stripping: the one who was worshiped by angels was spit upon by soldiers.
16.16: Job’s face reddened with weeping, deep darkness on his eyelids, describes the physical evidence of sustained grief. Isa 53:3: “a man of sorrows and acquainted with grief” (or “acquainted with sickness”). The “sorrows” of the Servant include the grief evidenced by reddened eyes and darkened lids; the suffering face is the face of the one who bore human grief as his own.
16.17: “Although there is no violence in my hands and my prayer is pure” — the declaration of innocence in the midst of suffering is Job’s most direct anticipation of Christ’s passion. 1 Pet 2:22: “he committed no sin, neither was deceit found in his mouth” (quoting Isa 53:9). Luke 23:4: Pilate declares “I find no guilt in this man.” Luke 23:41: the criminal on the cross says of Jesus, “this man has done nothing wrong.” Job’s innocent suffering is the most precise OT structural parallel to the innocent suffering of Christ.
16.18: “O earth, cover not my blood, and let my cry find no resting place!” — this is the OT’s most direct invocation of the blood-crying pattern. Gen 4:10: Abel’s blood cries from the ground. Heb 12:24: Christ’s blood “speaks a better word than the blood of Abel.” The blood of the innocent sufferer that cries from the earth for justice is fulfilled in the cross: Christ’s blood cries not for vengeance (Abel’s blood) but for forgiveness (Luke 23:34: “Father, forgive them”). The blood that finds no resting place until justice is done finds its resting place at the throne of grace.
16.19: “Even now, behold, my witness is in heaven, and he who testifies for me is on high” — the heavenly witness/advocate is one of Job’s most theologically anticipatory moments. Rom 8:34: “Christ Jesus is the one who died — more than that, who was raised — who is at the right hand of God, who indeed is interceding for us.” Heb 7:25: “he always lives to make intercession for them.” 1 John 2:1: “if anyone does sin, we have an advocate with the Father, Jesus Christ the righteous.” Job longs for what the NT declares has been fulfilled: the heavenly witness and advocate for the righteous is the risen and ascended Christ.
16.20: My friends scorn me; my eyes pour out tears to God. The combination of human scorn and prayer to God despite the scorn is the exact posture of Christ in Gethsemane: the disciples sleeping (human scorn by abandonment), Jesus praying with sweat like drops of blood (Luke 22:44). The sufferer whose friends fail him but who still pours out tears to God is the pattern of the intercessor-sufferer that the passion enacts on the cosmic scale.
16.21: “That he would argue the case of a man with God, as a son of man does with his neighbor” — Job’s longing for a mediator who can argue a man’s case before God is the longing that the incarnation fulfills. 1 Tim 2:5: “there is one God, and there is one mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus.” The “son of man” (ben adam) who argues a man’s case before God is the title Christ appropriates: he is the Son of Man who argues our case before the divine throne (Dan 7:13–14; Heb 9:24: Christ “entered into heaven itself, now to appear in the presence of God on our behalf”).
16.22: “For when a few years have come I shall go the way from which I shall not return” — the one-way journey to death. Christ voluntarily took this journey, entering death not by exhaustion of years but by deliberate surrender: John 10:17–18: “I lay down my life that I may take it up again. No one takes it from me, but I lay it down of my own accord.” The irrevocability of death that terrifies Job is the very thing Christ transformed: the one-way road through Sheol became the passage through which the firstborn from the dead led many sons to glory (Heb 2:10).

17.1: “My spirit is broken; my days are extinct; the graveyard is ready for me” — the total exhaustion of the one approaching death by suffering. Luke 23:46: “Father, into your hands I commit my spirit” — Christ’s final words from the cross are the broken spirit’s voluntary surrender to the Father. The “graves ready for me” is fulfilled in the borrowed tomb: the Son of Man had nowhere to lay his head (Luke 9:58) and no tomb of his own; the grave prepared for him was another’s (Matt 27:60: Joseph of Arimathea’s new tomb).
17.2: Mockers surrounding Job and the dwelling of his eye on their provocation describes the sustained contempt of those watching the suffering. Mark 15:31–32: “so also the chief priests with the scribes mocked him to one another, saying, ‘He saved others; he cannot save himself.’” The mocking audience at the cross is the community of mockers that Job describes; Jesus on the cross was surrounded by mockers from multiple directions (Mark 15:29 the passers-by; v31 the chief priests; v32 those crucified with him).
17.3: “Lay down a pledge for me with yourself; who is there who will put up security for me?” — the longing for a guarantor/surety is one of Job’s most significant messianic longings. Heb 7:22: “Jesus has become the guarantor (enguos) of a better covenant.” The surety that Job cannot find — someone who will pledge before God on his behalf — is the exact function that Christ performs as the guarantor of the new covenant. Christ’s own life is the deposit/pledge laid down with the Father on behalf of those who trust in him.
17.4: God closing the hearts of the friends from understanding, not letting them triumph — the divine hardening that accompanies the testing of the righteous sufferer. In the passion narrative, the hardening of the opponents was part of the divine plan: Mark 4:12 (the crowds hear but do not understand; Isa 6:9–10 is being fulfilled). The hardening of those who oppose the innocent sufferer is not an accident but part of the providential pattern that moves toward the sufferer’s vindication.
17.5: Denouncing friends for reward, with the eyes of his children failing, is the anti-type of the truly faithful friend. John 15:13: “greater love has no one than this, that someone lay down his life for his friends.” Jesus is the friend who does not denounce for reward but who lays down his life at the cost of his own suffering for his friends’ sake. The failure of Job’s friends to be faithful is the shadow that the true Friend casts forward.
17.6: Being made a byword (limashal) of the peoples, spit in the face, describes the total public humiliation of the righteous sufferer. Num 12:14 uses spitting in the face as the extreme expression of public disgrace. Matt 27:30: “and they spit on him and took the reed and struck him on the head.” The innocent sufferer who becomes a byword among the peoples is a prophetic type of the one whose name — “Jesus” — the world has used both as a curse and as the ground of salvation.
17.7: Eyes dim with grief, limbs like a shadow — the physical deterioration of the suffering one. Isa 52:14: “his appearance was so marred, beyond human semblance, and his form beyond that of the children of mankind.” The disfigured servant whose appearance is beyond human recognition is the extreme version of the dimming eyes and shadow-limbs of Job 17:7. The physical deterioration of the suffering innocent is theologically significant: suffering is not spiritualized but embodied in both Job and in Christ.
17.8: Upright men being appalled at the innocent one’s condition, the innocent stirred up against the godless — the shock of the righteous community at the innocent sufferer’s fate. The disciples’ confusion and grief at Christ’s death (Luke 24:21: “we had hoped that he was the one to redeem Israel”) is the fulfillment of this appalled-upright-men pattern: those who loved Jesus were appalled at what happened to him and could not initially understand that it was necessary.
17.9: “Yet the righteous holds to his way, and he who has clean hands grows stronger and stronger” — the perseverance of the righteous through testing. Heb 12:2–3: “looking to Jesus, the founder and perfecter of our faith, who for the joy that was set before him endured the cross, despising the shame, and is seated at the right hand of the throne of God. Consider him who endured from sinners such hostility against himself, so that you may not grow weary or fainthearted.” The strengthening of the clean-handed righteous through suffering is the pattern Christ both embodies and enables for those who follow him.
17.10: Job’s challenge to the friends to return and debate with him — he does not find a wise man among them — anticipates Christ’s silence before his accusers (Matt 27:14: “but he gave him no answer, not even to a single charge”) and the failure of any legitimate charge to stick. When genuine wisdom faces the wisdom of the conventional tradition, the conventional tradition runs out of substance while genuine wisdom remains.
17.11: Days past, plans torn apart, the desires of his heart broken off — the dissolution of all human plans through catastrophic suffering. Phil 3:7–8: “whatever gain I had, I counted as loss for the sake of Christ. Indeed, I count everything as loss because of the surpassing worth of knowing Christ Jesus my Lord.” The dissolution that Job experiences involuntarily, Paul experiences voluntarily — counting all plans and desires as broken off for the sake of Christ. Both arrive at the same posture: naked trust in God alone when all human plans have been shredded.
17.12: Turning night into day, light near in the face of darkness describes Job’s distorted experience of time in suffering: day becomes night, night becomes day, the normal markers of time dissolve. At the crucifixion, day literally became night: Matt 27:45: “from the sixth hour there was darkness over all the land until the ninth hour.” The cosmic sign of the crucifixion reversed the normal day/night order — the one who is the light of the world entered the darkness of death precisely at noon.
17.13: If Job’s hope is to go to Sheol, to make his bed in darkness, describes the only hope left for the one whose suffering has extinguished all earthly hope. Ps 139:8: “if I make my bed in Sheol, you are there.” Christ descended into death (Acts 2:27–31: “you will not abandon my soul to Hades”; Eph 4:9: “he also descended into the lower regions, the earth”). The bed in darkness that is the last hope is where Christ went voluntarily — and transformed it into the place from which resurrection comes.
17.14: “If I say to the pit, ‘You are my father’; or to the worm, ‘My mother’ or ‘My sister’—” describes the dissolution of all familial categories into the kinship of mortality. At the cross, Jesus redefined family: John 19:26–27 (the beloved disciple and Mary as mother/son), Mark 3:35 (“whoever does the will of God, he is my brother and sister and mother”). The dissolution of earthly family that Job describes is the precondition for the new family of the kingdom that Christ establishes.
17.15: “Where then is my hope? Who will see my hope?” — the complete extinction of earthly hope at the bottom of the suffering. 1 Pet 1:3: “blessed be the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ! According to his great mercy, he has caused us to be born again to a living hope through the resurrection of Jesus Christ from the dead.” The answer to Job’s question “where is my hope?” is: in the resurrection. The living hope does not appear until after the extinction of all earthly hope — a pattern that the cross makes literal.
17.16: Hope going down to the bars of Sheol, descending together into the dust — the final descent. Acts 2:27–31 (quoting Ps 16:10): “you will not abandon my soul to Hades, nor let your Holy One see corruption.” The hope that goes down to the bars of Sheol is precisely where Christ went — but the Holy One did not see corruption. Job’s hope descending into Sheol’s bars is the pattern; Christ’s descent into death that did not end in corruption is the fulfillment that transforms the descent into the gateway of resurrection.

18.1: Bildad’s second speech begins with a rhetorical challenge to Job to let the speech end so the friends can speak. In the passion narrative, the crowds similarly shouted down the voice of the innocent sufferer (Mark 15:13–14: they cried out even more, “crucify him”; Pilate tried to argue but was shouted down). The pressure to silence the one who insists on innocence in the face of conventional judgment is a constant feature of the innocent sufferer’s situation.
18.2: Trapping with words before understanding (“how long will you hunt for words”) uses the hunting metaphor for Bildad’s argumentative strategy. In the passion narrative, the Pharisees and Herodians similarly “sent to him some of their disciples and some of the Herodians to entangle him in his talk” (Matt 22:15–16). The trap-setting opponents who look for the damaging word are a constant feature of the innocent sufferer’s legal persecution.
18.3: Why are we counted as cattle and stupid in Job’s sight? — Bildad’s wounded pride at being called unwise by the sufferer he is supposedly helping. The religious authorities similarly resented Jesus’ implicit and explicit critique of their wisdom: Luke 20:19 (“the scribes and the chief priests sought to lay hands on him at that very hour, for they perceived that he had told this parable against them”). The reversal of wisdom-assessment — the supposedly wise calling the sufferer stupid while the sufferer correctly evaluates the wise as cattle-dumb — is the structural irony of both Job and the passion.
18.4: Job in tearing himself in his anger — shall the earth be forsaken for you, and the rock be removed out of its place? — describes the friends’ accusation that Job’s suffering is self-inflicted. At the cross, the accusation that Jesus’ suffering was self-caused (“he saved others; let him save himself,” Luke 23:35) is the same logic. The “rock removed from its place” anticipates the stone that was rolled away from the tomb (Matt 28:2) — the rock that the friends said could not be moved for Job’s sake was moved at Christ’s resurrection.
18.5: The wicked man’s light goes out (gam ’or resha’im yid’ak) and his flame does not shine. Matt 27:45: “now from the sixth hour there was darkness over all the land until the ninth hour.” The darkness at the crucifixion was the cosmic sign of divine judgment falling upon the sin-bearer; the light going out is both the literal darkness of the three hours and the symbolic judgment on the sin that Christ bore. Bildad describes the fate of the wicked; the crucifixion darkness shows this judgment falling on Christ who bore the wicked’s penalty.
18.6: The light in his tent grows dark and his lamp above him is put out — the extinction of the domestic light as the symbol of hope and presence. John 8:12: “I am the light of the world. Whoever follows me will not walk in darkness, but will have the light of life.” The progressive extinction of the wicked man’s light is the anti-type of Christ, the light that darkness cannot extinguish. The lamp put out in judgment on the wicked is the same darkness that Christ enters voluntarily — and conquers.
18.7: The vigorous stride of the wicked shortening, his own counsel throwing him down, describes the self-destruction of the proud. Prov 16:18: “pride goes before destruction, and a haughty spirit before a fall.” The structural contrast with Christ: his humility (“I am gentle and lowly in heart,” Matt 11:29) is the anti-type of the wicked one’s shortened stride. Christ’s deliberate lowering of himself (Phil 2:7–8) is the voluntary inversion of the wicked man’s pride-before-fall.
18.8: The wicked man walking into a net, stepping into a web — the accumulation of traps. In the passion narrative, the arrest is framed as a capture: Mark 14:44 (the signal to arrest Jesus, “seize him”); Luke 22:54 (“they seized him and led him away”). But the irony of the arrest is that the one who was “seized” had already freely submitted: John 10:18 (“no one takes it from me, but I lay it down of my own accord”). The trap that catches the wicked is entered voluntarily by Christ as the necessary path through death.
18.9: A snare seizing by the heel (yahazez be’aqev) and a trap holding him fast uses the heel imagery that reaches back to Gen 3:15 (the serpent striking the heel of the woman’s offspring). The messianic promise is that the seed of the woman will be struck in the heel but will crush the serpent’s head. The “heel-seizing” (’aqev, the root of “Jacob”/supplanter) that catches the wicked is, in the messianic reading, the wound Christ received at the cross — the fatal blow that was not ultimately fatal, because the head-crusher prevailed.
18.10: The rope hidden in the ground for the wicked, the trap in the path, continues the multi-layered snare imagery. In the passion narrative, the plot against Jesus was hidden and multi-layered: John 11:53 (“from that day on they made plans to put him to death”); the betrayal of Judas was the specific trap in the path (Luke 22:3–6: “Satan entered into Judas…and he went away and conferred with the chief priests and officers how he might betray him”). The hidden rope and the path-trap were the mechanisms of the passion.
18.11: Terrors frightening the wicked on every side (sevivav biehatu ballahot) scattering at his feet describes the psychological state of the wicked at judgment. Gethsemane inverts this: it is Christ who is surrounded by terrors (“he began to be sorrowful and troubled…my soul is very sorrowful, even to death,” Matt 26:37–38). Christ voluntarily entered the terrors that belong to the guilty; the terrors that scatter the wicked at their feet are the terrors Christ bore in the garden before the cross.
18.12: Calamity hungry for the wicked and disaster ready at his side describes the constant attendance of doom. In the passion, “this is the hour” (John 12:27; Luke 22:53) was the moment when the calamity that had been attending arrived. Christ knew the hour was coming and moved deliberately toward it: John 12:23 (“the hour has come for the Son of Man to be glorified”). The calamity that is “hungry” for the wicked is the judgment that was satisfied at Calvary by the one who took it voluntarily.
18.13: Eating the parts of his skin, eating his limbs, the firstborn of death devouring him — disease consuming the body. The “firstborn of death” (bekhoro mavet) is a personification of the most lethal disease or, typologically, death’s primary offspring. Heb 2:14–15: Christ “through death destroyed the one who has the power of death, that is, the devil, and delivered all those who through fear of death were subject to lifelong slavery.” The firstborn of death that devours the wicked is the power that Christ’s death destroyed; his resurrection was the definitive defeat of the firstborn of death.
18.14: “He is torn from the tent in which he trusted and is marched before the king of terrors” (malkey ballahot) — the king of terrors is the personification of death. 1 Cor 15:55: “O death, where is your victory? O death, where is your sting?” (quoting Hos 13:14). The king of terrors before whom the wicked is marched is the death that Christ faced on behalf of the wicked; Col 2:15: “he disarmed the rulers and authorities and put them to open shame, by triumphing over them in him.” The triumphant march before the king of terrors is, in Christ’s case, reversed: the king of terrors is marched in Christ’s triumphal procession.
18.15: Nothing of his dwelling remaining, sulfur scattered over his habitation — the complete desolation. The sulfur (gofrit) evokes Sodom and Gomorrah (Gen 19:24: sulfur and fire from YHWH) as the paradigm of complete divine judgment. Christ took the Sodom-judgment upon himself at Calvary (the “cup” of divine wrath, Mark 14:36) so that those who trust in him escape the desolation. The complete desolation of the wicked’s dwelling is the judgment that fell on Christ at Golgotha.
18.16: Roots drying up below and branches withering above — the complete plant-death from root to tip. John 15:6: “whoever does not abide in me is thrown away like a branch and withers.” The drying-up from roots describes separation from the source of life. Christ as the true Vine (John 15:1) is the answer to the withered branches of the wicked: those who abide in him are branches that do not wither, precisely because their roots draw from the life that conquered death.
18.17: Memory of the wicked perishing from the earth, no name remaining in the streets. Ps 112:6: “the righteous will never be moved; he will be remembered forever.” The contrast is complete: the wicked’s memory perishes; the righteous is remembered forever. Christ’s name has spread to every street of every city of every nation (Acts 1:8: “to the ends of the earth”); the one who was crucified between criminals has the name above every name (Phil 2:9–11). The perishing memory of the wicked is the anti-type of the eternal name of Christ.
18.18: Thrust from light into darkness, driven out of the world — the complete spatial displacement of the wicked. Christ’s entry into darkness at the cross was voluntary rather than forced: John 13:30 (“Judas went out; and it was night”) describes the outer darkness that Judas entered; Christ entered that same darkness as the light-bearer who would transform it (John 1:5: “the light shines in the darkness, and the darkness has not overcome it”). The darkness from which the wicked cannot return is the darkness Christ entered and exited in resurrection.
18.19: No offspring or posterity among his people, no survivor where he sojourned. Isa 53:8 asks “as for his generation, who considered that he was cut off out of the land of the living?” and v10 answers: “he shall see his offspring, he shall prolong his days.” The apparent childlessness of the cut-off servant is resolved by the servant’s spiritual offspring: all who receive him he gives “the right to become children of God” (John 1:12). The wicked’s offspring-extinction is the anti-type of the servant who “sees his offspring” in the many sons brought to glory (Heb 2:10).
18.20: Those in the west appalled at the wicked man’s fate, those in the east seized with horror — the universal reverberation of the wicked one’s catastrophic end. The crucifixion similarly reverberated universally: the earthquake (Matt 27:51–54), the tearing of the temple veil, the centurion’s confession (“truly this was the Son of God!”). The catastrophe at Golgotha sent reverberations east and west that have continued to reach the ends of the earth.
18.21: “Surely such are the dwellings of the unrighteous, such is the place of him who knows not God” — Bildad’s summary verdict. Ironically, the cross — the place of execution that the Romans designed as the ultimate place of the unrighteous — became the place where God was most fully known. John 12:32: “and I, when I am lifted up from the earth, will draw all people to myself.” The place of the unrighteous became, in Christ’s occupation of it, the place where the unrighteous find God — the ultimate typological reversal.

5.1: Eliphaz challenges Job to call out to any of the holy ones. No mediator is available to him — a lack the book will resolve only in ch. 19 where Job cries for his gōʾēl and ch. 33 where Elihu names the heavenly meliṣ. Christ is the one Mediator who answers every cry (1 Tim 2:5).
5.2: Vexation kills the fool and jealousy slays the simple. Eliphaz’s wisdom tradition locates the source of suffering in the sufferer. Christ absorbs vexation from without, bearing the contradiction of sinners (Heb 12:3), demonstrating that affliction is not always self-generated.
5.3: Eliphaz’s cursed-root image anticipates the fig-tree judgment (Matt 21:19) and John 15:6 (branches that do not abide are thrown away). The righteous flourishing instead of the wicked finds its ground only in union with Christ the true Vine.
5.4: The sons of the fool are crushed in court with no defender. Christ’s redemptive work is precisely that of the advocate in the gate (1 John 2:1; Ps 82:3–4), lifting up the defenseless who have no human rescuer.
5.5: The harvest devoured by the hungry, wealth taken by thorns. The imagery of cursed ground and lost harvest traces to Gen 3:17–18. Christ redeems the curse (Gal 3:13), restoring the creation’s fruitfulness in the kingdom he inaugurates.
5.6: Affliction does not spring from the dust nor does trouble sprout from the ground. Eliphaz’s point cuts both ways: suffering has a cause, but in Christ’s case that cause is entirely outside himself — he bears what he did not sow (Ps 69:4).
5.7: “Man is born to trouble as the sparks fly upward.” The Incarnation is God entering the trajectory of human suffering. Isaiah 53:3 calls the Servant “a man of sorrows and acquainted with grief” — Christ fulfills the destiny of mortal humanity by taking its trouble onto himself completely.
5.8: Eliphaz counsels seeking God and committing one’s cause to him. This is precisely what Jesus does in Gethsemane and on the cross (Luke 23:46; Ps 22:1) — the perfect Son who always commits his cause to the Father, even in extremis.
5.9: God does great and unsearchable things, marvelous without number. Paul cites this doxological register in Rom 11:33 after explaining the mystery of redemption. The unsearchable things find their apex in the cross: “depth of riches both of the wisdom and knowledge of God” (Rom 11:33).
5.10: God sends rain on the earth and water on the fields. Jesus cites this same universal providence in Matt 5:45: the Father causes rain to fall on the just and unjust alike. Common grace points toward the greater grace of the gospel offered to all people.
5.11: He sets on high those who are lowly, and mourners are lifted to safety. Mary’s Magnificat echoes this text (Luke 1:52). Christ’s entire ministry enacts this reversal: the Beatitudes, the raising of the lowly, the healing of mourners.
5.12: God frustrates the devices of the crafty so their hands achieve no success. This divine reversal of human cunning runs through the passion narrative: the schemes of priests and Pharisees to destroy Jesus become the very means God uses to accomplish redemption (Acts 2:23).
5.13: “He catches the wise in their own craftiness” — directly quoted in 1 Cor 3:19. Paul applies this to those who boast in human wisdom against the “foolishness” of the cross. The crucified Christ is God’s trap for every wisdom that exalts itself above the knowledge of God (2 Cor 10:5).
5.14: The crafty meet darkness at noon. The crucifixion darkness (Matt 27:45; Amos 8:9) is the cosmic sign that the world’s wisdom has met its noon-darkness — unable to see the light of the resurrection that follows.
5.15: God saves the needy from the sword of their mouth and from the hand of the mighty. Christ’s trial before Pilate is the paradigm case: the sword of accusation was at its sharpest, yet God vindicates through resurrection the one who had no earthly defender.
5.16: The poor have hope, and injustice shuts its mouth. The hope of the poor reaches its foundation in the resurrection of Christ (1 Pet 1:3), who is raised as the firstfruits of a new order in which injustice is finally silenced at the judgment.
5.17: “Blessed is the one whom God reproves; do not despise the Almighty’s discipline.” Cited verbatim in Heb 12:5–6 (also Prov 3:11–12), applied to Christian suffering as fatherly correction. The logic is Christological: Christ himself “learned obedience through what he suffered” (Heb 5:8), modeling the way sonship and suffering coinhere.
5.18: “He wounds, but he binds up; he shatters, but his hands heal.” Hos 6:1–2 draws on this pattern and culminates in “after two days he will revive us; on the third day he will raise us up” — language Paul reads as a resurrection template (1 Cor 15:4). Peter quotes the same wound-and-heal dynamic and applies it to Christ’s cross: “by his wounds you have been healed” (1 Pet 2:24).
5.19: Deliverance from six troubles, even seven. The fullness of deliverance Eliphaz sketches (six-then-seven = completeness) is what Christ actually accomplishes: he delivers from every trouble enumerated — death, famine, war, slander, destruction — through his redemptive work.
5.20: In famine he redeems from death; in war from the power of the sword. The gōʾēl-redemption language used here previews Job’s own cry in 19:25 and finds its referent in Christ who “redeems us from the curse of the law” (Gal 3:13) and from the power of death (Heb 2:14–15).
5.21: Hidden from the scourge of the tongue, not afraid of destruction when it comes. The hiddenness of God’s people under trial — Col 3:3 (“your life is hidden with Christ in God”) — is grounded in Christ’s own hiddenness in the tomb before the resurrection.
5.22: Laugh at destruction and famine; not afraid of wild animals. This fearlessness before death finds its warrant only in the resurrection. Paul’s “death is swallowed up in victory — where, O death, is your sting?” (1 Cor 15:54–55) is the christological fulfillment of what Eliphaz can only idealize.
5.23: A covenant with the stones of the field; the wild animals at peace. The Edenic peace Eliphaz promises is the new-creation peace inaugurated by Christ, the second Adam, who restores the harmonious dominion lost in the fall (Isa 11:6–9; Rom 8:21).
5.24: Know that your tent is at peace. Christ promises a place prepared (John 14:2–3) — the dwelling-place security that Eliphaz dangles conditionally is given as gift to those united to Christ.
5.25: Offspring will be many; descendants as the grass of the earth. The Abrahamic promise of seed (Gen 17:6) is fulfilled in Christ who becomes the ancestor of “many children” (Heb 2:10) through his redemptive work: “he shall see his offspring” (Isa 53:10).
5.26: Come to the grave in ripe old age, like a shock of grain. The resurrection of the body grants what Eliphaz could only imagine — not escape from death but its transformation into harvest (1 Cor 15:37–38, 42–44, the grain metaphor).
5.27: “This we have searched out; it is true.” Eliphaz’s confidence in inherited tradition is real but incomplete: it takes the revelation of the cross to make full sense of the wound-that-heals pattern he has rightly observed. The gospel is the deeper logic beneath the wisdom tradition.

6.1: Job speaks — he does not stay silent. The cry of the suffering one that opens this speech finds its archetype in the Psalms of lament (Ps 22:1; 55:2) and its ultimate voice in Christ’s cry from the cross: the eternal Son enters the full register of human anguish.
6.2: Job’s vexation and calamity are beyond weighing. The weight of suffering here anticipates Paul’s “momentary light affliction” language (2 Cor 4:17) — paradoxical because Christ’s cross was the heaviest weight ever borne, yet its eternal purchase infinitely outweighs it.
6.3: Heavier than the sand of the seas — therefore his words are rash. Christ bore inexpressible weight without rash words (1 Pet 2:23: “he did not revile in return”), fulfilling what Job could not: perfect speech under maximal suffering.
6.4: “The arrows of the Almighty are in me; my spirit drinks their poison; the terrors of God are arrayed against me.” This arrow-of-divine-wrath language echoes Ps 38:2 and Ps 22:15. At the cross Christ bears the full quiver of divine judgment: “It was the will of the LORD to crush him” (Isa 53:10). The terrors arrayed against Job are a shadow of what Christ absorbed completely.
6.5: The donkey does not bray when it has grass. Job’s lament is proportional to his lack — he is not complaining from surplus. Christ’s cry from the cross (“My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?”) is likewise no performative complaint but the exact expression of genuine desolation.
6.6: Can tasteless food be eaten without salt? Job refuses to pretend false words are nourishment. Christ offers himself as the true bread that satisfies (John 6:35), in contrast to the tasteless offering of inadequate comfort.
6.7: The soul refuses to touch loathsome food. Christ took on what was loathsome to God — sin itself — so that his people might receive what is truly nourishing: his righteousness (2 Cor 5:21).
6.8: Oh that my request would come and God would grant what I hope for. The prayer of desperate longing is perfected in Gethsemane: “Not my will but yours be done” (Luke 22:42). Christ models the transformation of desperate request into submission.
6.9: That it would please God to crush me, to let loose his hand and cut me off. The desire for the end of suffering finds its answer not in annihilation but in the cross: God did crush the Servant (Isa 53:10) so that death for his people would mean passage through into resurrection.
6.10: Consolation: I have not denied the words of the Holy One. Job’s integrity under pressure — not denying the Holy One — is the posture Christ maintains throughout his trial and crucifixion: he does not recant or curse, vindicating the Father’s trust (John 17:4).
6.11: What is my strength that I should wait? The exhausted servant who can no longer hope from personal reserves finds his resources replaced: “They who wait for the LORD shall renew their strength” (Isa 40:31). Christ, raised by the Spirit (Rom 8:11), is the source of that renewed strength.
6.12: Is my strength the strength of stones? Is my flesh bronze? Job names the human limits that make endurance impossible by natural resources. Christ assumes full human fragility (John 11:35; Heb 5:7–8) and transforms it by resurrection — the weakness of the cross becomes the power of God (1 Cor 1:25).
6.13: Is not my help absent from me? All resources driven away. This absolute dereliction is the condition Christ enters on the cross: “My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?” (Matt 27:46). The absence of creaturely help is the condition that necessitates redemption from God alone.
6.14: “He who withholds kindness from a friend forsakes the fear of the Almighty.” The ḥeseḍ — steadfast covenant love — that the friends fail to show points to the one who showed it perfectly: “Greater love has no one than this, that someone lay down his life for his friends” (John 15:13). Christ is the friend who never withholds ḥeseḍ.
6.15: Brothers treacherous as wadis that overflow. Betrayal by close companions characterizes both Job’s situation and Christ’s: Judas, Peter’s denial, the disciples’ flight (Mark 14:50). The treachery of intimate friends is part of the Suffering Servant’s lot (Ps 41:9, cited in John 13:18).
6.16: The dark streams from ice, snow’s melt vanishing. What seemed like reliable water is gone. The fickle support mirrors the crowds of Palm Sunday who became the crowds crying “Crucify him” — apparent backing that dissolves at the moment of need.
6.17: When it is warm they vanish; when hot, they disappear from their place. The unreliability of human support under pressure makes the faithful God all the more necessary. Jesus “knew what was in man” (John 2:25) and did not entrust himself to human loyalty — a realism confirmed at every point in the passion.
6.18: Caravans turn aside; they go up into the waste and perish. Those who count on empty promises find only desert. Christ offers living water (John 4:14) in place of the cisterns that fail (Jer 2:13) — the only reliable source when all earthly streams dry up.
6.19: The caravans of Tema look; travelers of Sheba hope. The nations seeking water represent the universal human longing — a longing Christ satisfies: “Let anyone who is thirsty come to me and drink” (John 7:37), the fulfillment of what every searching caravan ultimately sought.
6.20: They are ashamed because they trusted; they come and are disappointed. False hope leads to shame — which is why Paul writes “hope does not put us to shame” (Rom 5:5) — because the love of God has been poured into our hearts through Christ, whose resurrection proves this hope is not empty.
6.21: “You have become nothing; you see my calamity and are afraid.” The disciples before the cross shrank back (Mark 14:50); the crowds turned; the friends became nothing. Christ’s isolation at the cross is total — bearing the abandonment Job experienced so that his people would never face it ultimately (Heb 13:5).
6.22: Have I said, give me a gift from your wealth? Job asks nothing of them. Nor does Christ demand that his disciples produce what they cannot give — his redemptive work is entirely his own act, not a collective achievement (Isa 63:3: “I trod the winepress alone”).
6.23: Deliver me from the enemy’s hand; ransom me from the hand of the ruthless? Job asks only for presence and truth, not resources. Christ is himself the ransom (Mark 10:45, lytron) — the one who delivers from the hand of the truly ruthless enemy, death and the devil.
6.24: Teach me and I will be silent; make me understand how I have gone astray. The posture of teachability even in suffering — Christ models this in full submission to the Father’s word, not silencing his cry but ultimately yielding: “Not my will but yours” (Luke 22:42).
6.25: How painful are upright words. Christ receives the full weight of human accusation — false and malicious — without being broken by it (1 Pet 2:23). His silence before accusers (Mark 15:5) fulfills Isa 53:7.
6.26: Do you think to reprove words when the speech of a despairing man is wind? The friends judge Job’s cries by their worst possible interpretation. Christ does not despise the cry of the afflicted (Ps 22:24) — the incarnate God who himself cried out never dismisses human anguish as mere noise.
6.27: You would even cast lots over the orphan. The imagery of casting lots over a defenseless one anticipates the soldiers casting lots over Christ’s garments at the crucifixion (John 19:24, fulfilling Ps 22:18) — the ultimate moment of treating the innocent one as abandoned property.
6.28: Look at me and see. The demand to be truly seen — not reduced to a category — is the cry Christ answers: he sees Zacchaeus in the tree, the woman in the crowd, the thief on the cross. The incarnate Lord is the God who truly looks at people (John 1:42, 48).
6.29: “Please turn; my vindication (ṣidqî) is at stake.” Job’s righteousness is on the line in the friends’ accusation. Christ’s resurrection is his vindication — “vindicated by the Spirit” (1 Tim 3:16). The resurrection is God’s “turn” that declares the righteous one innocent.
6.30: Is there any injustice on my tongue? The claim of innocence that Job cannot fully sustain (9:2) Christ can sustain absolutely: he is the one in whose mouth no deceit was found (1 Pet 2:22, citing Isa 53:9). His vindication is the basis on which the guilty receive righteousness.

7.1: “Has not man hard service (ṣābāʾ) on earth?” The term ṣābāʾ denotes military conscription, forced labor, a compulsory term of hard service. Christ came not to be served but to serve (Mark 10:45, using the same service-language), entering human ṣābāʾ to redeem those under it. He bore the full weight of mortal toil in order to transform it.
7.2: Like a slave longing for shadow, a hired worker for his wages. The longing for rest from labor finds its answer in Christ: “Come to me, all who labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest” (Matt 11:28). Christ is the Sabbath-rest that the slave in v. 2 can never reach by toil alone.
7.3: So I am allotted months of emptiness, nights of misery. Christ descended into the full emptiness of human experience — the kenōsis of Phil 2:7. He did not hover above human misery but was allotted its months as truly as any sufferer.
7.4: When I lie down: when shall I arise? The long night of suffering that makes morning seem impossible — the two days of the disciples’ grief before the resurrection — is the dark night Job articulates. Easter morning is God’s answer to every sufferer’s “when shall I arise?”
7.5: Flesh clothed with worms and clods of dirt; skin hardened and breaks out afresh. The bodily degradation Job describes is the condition of mortality that Christ assumes — including the crucified body laid in a tomb. The resurrection transforms this exact flesh (1 Cor 15:42–44: sown in corruption, raised in glory).
7.6: Days swifter than a weaver’s shuttle, consumed without hope. The brevity and hopelessness of mortal life is precisely what Christ enters by the Incarnation — taking up finite, fleeting human days — and redeems by resurrection: his risen life is eternal, lifting human days into something beyond the shuttle’s vanishing.
7.7: “Remember that my life is a breath; my eye will not again see good.” Job’s prayer for divine remembrance is answered in Christ: God does not forget the dying one (Luke 23:42–43, the thief on the cross: “remember me” — “today you will be with me”). The breath of human life is not forgotten by the Creator.
7.8: The eye of him who sees me will behold me no more; your eyes will be on me but I will be gone. The fear of disappearing from God’s sight is inverted by the resurrection: Christ, raised and ascended, is permanently in the Father’s sight as glorified humanity. Those in Christ share that permanent vision (John 17:24).
7.9: “As the cloud fades and vanishes, so he who goes down to Sheol does not come up.” This is Job’s bleak anthropology of one-way descent. Peter’s Pentecost sermon cites Ps 16:10 precisely to say that Christ was not abandoned to Hades (Acts 2:24, 27, 31) — he is the exception to the rule Job articulates. The God of Job’s desperate hope raised Christ, breaking the irreversibility Job feared.
7.10: He returns no more to his house; his place knows him no more. The finality of death as Job sees it is what the resurrection overturns. Christ returns to his disciples after three days (John 20:19) — his place does know him again — inaugurating the pattern for the resurrection of the dead.
7.11: Therefore I will not restrain my mouth; I will speak in the anguish of my spirit. The full articulation of anguish — not suppressed, not performative — is what Heb 5:7 says Jesus offered: “In the days of his flesh, Jesus offered up prayers and supplications, with loud cries and tears.” Christ is the one who speaks in the anguish of his spirit before the Father.
