23.10: “But he knows the way that I take; when he has tested me, I will come out as gold” — one of Job’s most confident expressions of faith through testing. 1 Pet 1:6–7: “in this you rejoice, though now for a little while, if necessary, you have been grieved by various trials, so that the tested genuineness of your faith — more precious than gold that perishes though it is tested by fire — may be found to result in praise and glory and honor at the revelation of Jesus Christ.” Job’s gold-trial typology is the exact metaphor Peter applies to the faith-testing of Christians.
23.11: My foot has held fast to his steps; I have kept his way and have not turned aside — the perseverance of the righteous on the appointed path. Heb 12:1–2: “let us run with endurance the race that is set before us, looking to Jesus, the founder and perfecter of our faith, who for the joy that was set before him endured the cross.” The one who held fast to God’s steps without turning aside is the pattern; Christ is the ultimate fulfillment — the one who ran the full race without deviation.
23.12: Treasuring the words of God’s mouth more than appointed food — the divine word as superior to physical sustenance. Matt 4:4: “man shall not live by bread alone, but by every word that comes from the mouth of God” (quoting Deut 8:3). Christ spoke this principle against the tempter during his own wilderness fast; he lived it through his entire ministry; and he became it: “I am the bread of life” (John 6:35) — the word of God that is itself the living bread.
23.13: “But he is unchangeable, and who can turn him back?” — the immutability of the divine plan. Heb 13:8: “Jesus Christ is the same yesterday and today and forever.” Jas 1:17: “every good gift and every perfect gift is from above, coming down from the Father of lights, with whom there is no variation or shadow due to change.” The unchangeability that terrifies Job is the unchangeability of the one who has committed to his eternal covenant in Christ: his plan to redeem cannot be turned back.
23.14: God completing his decree against Job — the sovereign divine plan carried through. Acts 2:23: “this Jesus, delivered up according to the definite plan and foreknowledge of God.” The divine decree that feels to Job like relentless divine determination against him is, in the NT, the relentless divine determination for human redemption, expressed supremely in the cross. The “many such things” that God has decreed include the passion, resurrection, and restoration of all who are in Christ.
23.15: Being terrified at God’s presence, dismayed when considering him — the overwhelming awe of divine encounter. Rev 1:17: John the Apostle falls at Christ’s feet as though dead at the vision of the risen Christ — and Christ lays his right hand on him and says, “Fear not, I am the first and the last, and the living one.” The terror of divine encounter is real; Christ’s “fear not” is the specific word that transforms the encounter from annihilating dread to life-giving meeting.
23.16: God making Job’s heart faint, the Almighty terrifying him — the divine action that produces the deepest dread. Luke 22:41–44: Christ’s sweat like drops of blood in Gethsemane is the most extreme example of a heart made faint by divine dealing. The one who experienced the fullest measure of the terror that Job describes is Christ facing the cup of divine wrath; having drunk it, he could say “let not your hearts be troubled” (John 14:1) to those who trust in his completed work.
23.17: Job not silenced by darkness or by the thick darkness that covers his face — the persistence of faith through darkness. Rom 8:35–37: “who shall separate us from the love of Christ? Shall tribulation, or distress, or persecution, or famine, or nakedness, or danger, or sword? … No, in all these things we are more than conquerors through him who loved us.” Job’s refusal to be silenced by darkness is the OT anticipation of the “more than conquerors” confidence of those who know that nothing can separate them from Christ’s love.

24.1: “Why are times not kept by the Almighty?” — Job’s demand for divine scheduling of justice. 2 Pet 3:8–9: “with the Lord one day is as a thousand years, and a thousand years as one day. The Lord is not slow to fulfill his promise as some count slowness, but is patient toward you, not wishing that any should perish, but that all should reach repentance.” The delay of justice that Job finds incomprehensible is the patience of God aimed at repentance; Rev 22:12: “behold, I am coming soon.” The “times” are set; Christ returns on the Father’s schedule.
24.2: Moving boundary stones — the paradigmatic social crime of the ancient world. In the NT, this crime’s spiritual analog is teaching that moves the eternal boundaries of God’s moral law: Matt 5:17 (“do not think that I have come to abolish the Law or the Prophets; I have not come to abolish them but to fulfill them”). Christ is the one who honors and fulfills the permanent boundaries of God’s law, while the one who moves those boundaries is under judgment (Gal 1:8–9).
24.3: Stealing the widow’s ox and the orphan’s donkey — predatory exploitation of the vulnerable. The Incarnation’s specific social context: Jesus was born into poverty (Luke 2:7), lived as a craftsman’s son, associated deliberately with the poor and marginalized. The one who was himself socially marginal (no permanent home, Matt 8:20; executed between criminals) is the countertype to the predator who steals from the widow and orphan.
24.4: The poor of the earth hiding together from oppression — the underground existence of the dispossessed. Jesus’ beatitude: “blessed are the meek (praeis, the poor and dispossessed), for they shall inherit the earth” (Matt 5:5). The poor who hide now will receive the inheritance; the eschatological reversal of Job 24 is the full kingdom that Christ inaugurates and will consummate.
24.5: The dispossessed poor like wild donkeys foraging in the wilderness — marginal existence outside the structures of social security. The wilderness was also where Jesus was tested (Matt 4:1–11), where the disciples were sent (Mark 6:31–32 for rest), and where John the Baptist proclaimed the way of the Lord (Luke 3:4). The wild-donkey existence of the marginalized is the world Christ deliberately entered in his incarnation and ministry.
24.6: Gathering fodder in another’s field, gleaning in the vineyard of the wicked — the survival economics of the dispossessed. Ruth gleaned in Boaz’s field (Ruth 2); Jesus’ disciples plucked grain on the Sabbath because they were hungry (Matt 12:1). The gleaner in another’s field is the economic precarity that the kingdom of God will transform: the kingdom feast where all are invited and all eat until satisfied (Matt 22:9–10; Luke 14:21–23).
24.7: The naked sleeping without clothing through the night, with no covering in the cold — material deprivation that fails human dignity. Matt 25:36,38: “I was naked and you clothed me.” The eschatological judgment turns on how the community treated the naked: in serving the naked, they served Christ himself. The naked who have no covering are precisely the ones in whom Christ says he was present; clothing them is clothing him.
24.8: Wet with mountain rains, clinging to the rock for want of shelter — exposure without protection. The rock as the only available shelter for the destitute is the theological image of God as rock (Ps 18:2; 31:3; 62:2). Christ became the permanent shelter for the spiritually destitute: “he who dwells in the shelter of the Most High will abide in the shadow of the Almighty” (Ps 91:1); Rev 7:16: “they shall hunger no more, neither thirst any more; the sun shall not strike them, nor any scorching heat.”
24.9: Orphans snatched from the breast, infants of the poor taken as pledges — child exploitation for debt. The Christ who said “let the little children come to me and do not hinder them, for to such belongs the kingdom of heaven” (Matt 19:14) is the one who specifically protects and receives those whom the economic system exploits. The children snatched for debt are the countertype of the children welcomed to the kingdom.
24.10: Going about naked, hungry, carrying the sheaves of grain they produce but cannot eat — the exploitation economy where workers starve amid their own production. The Lord’s Supper specifically addresses this inversion: 1 Cor 11:21 (the rich eat and drink while the poor go hungry at the same table). Christ’s institution of the common meal is the specific counter to the economic inversion Job describes.
24.11: Treading winepresses while thirsty — producing abundance while experiencing deprivation. John 4:13–14: “everyone who drinks of this water will be thirsty again, but whoever drinks of the water that I will give him will never be thirsty again.” The workers who tread wine while thirsty find their thirst permanently satisfied in Christ; he who said “I thirst” from the cross (John 19:28) bore the thirst so that those who come to him need never thirst again.
24.12: “From the city the dying groan, and the soul of the wounded cries for help; yet God charges no one with wrongdoing” — the cry of the oppressed without divine response. Luke 18:7–8: “will not God give justice to his elect who cry to him day and night? Will he delay long over them? I tell you, he will give justice to them speedily.” The cry that appears unanswered in Job 24:12 receives Christ’s direct promise: the divine response is coming speedily. The apparent divine indifference is the patience before the eschatological judgment.
24.13: Those who rebel against the light — the moral taxonomy of light-haters. John 3:19–20: “people loved the darkness rather than the light because their works were evil. For everyone who does wicked things hates the light and does not come to the light, lest his works should be exposed.” The light-rebelling wicked of Job 24:13 are precisely the ones who flee the light of Christ; the darkness they prefer is the darkness from which Christ has come to deliver them (1 Pet 2:9: “called out of darkness into his marvelous light”).
24.14: The murderer rising at dawn to kill the poor — the exploitation of the transitional moment. Christ’s resurrection at dawn (John 20:1: “early on the first day of the week, while it was still dark”) is the ultimate transformation of the dawn: the time when the murderer rises to kill becomes the time when the Life-giver rises from death. The darkness that was the murderer’s ally is permanently defeated by the light of resurrection dawn.
24.15: The adulterer waiting for twilight — the moral coding of darkness as concealment. Eph 5:11–13: “take no part in the unfruitful works of darkness, but instead expose them. For it is shameful even to speak of the things that they do in secret. But when anything is exposed by the light, it becomes visible.” Christ as the light that exposes darkness (John 3:20–21) is the specific answer to the darkness-hiding that the adulterer practices: nothing is hidden from the one who is the light of the world.
24.16: The thief sealing himself in darkness, not knowing the light — the complete severance from the light. 1 Thess 5:4–5: “but you are not in darkness, brothers, for that day to surprise you like a thief. For you are all children of light, children of the day. We are not of the night or of the darkness.” Christ transforms his followers from darkness-children into light-children; the thief who seals himself in darkness is the anti-type of the community that has been transferred from darkness’ dominion to the kingdom of light (Col 1:13).
24.17: Thick darkness as morning to the light-haters — the complete inversion of the diurnal moral order. At the crucifixion, the moral inversion reached its maximum: darkness at noon (Matt 27:45), the Light of the World extinguished in apparent defeat. But the Easter morning reversed this: darkness became dawn, death became life. The permanent inversion that the wicked inhabit was temporarily entered by Christ and definitively overturned.
24.18: The wicked swift on the water’s surface, their portion cursed in the land — the lightness of the wicked who have no substantial root. John 15:6: “whoever does not abide in me is thrown away like a branch and withers.” The rootless wicked who skim the surface are the anti-type of those who abide in Christ, the true Vine, and draw sustenance from his life.
24.19: Drought and heat consuming the snow waters, Sheol taking those who have sinned — the irreversible movement of the unrooted toward death. 1 Cor 15:55–57: “O death, where is your victory? O death, where is your sting? The sting of death is sin, and the power of sin is the law. But thanks be to God, who gives us the victory through our Lord Jesus Christ.” The Sheol-consumption that is the end of the sinner is defeated by Christ’s death and resurrection; the sting is removed for those who are his.
24.20: The womb forgotten, worms feasting on them, no longer remembered — the complete extinction. Heb 11:4: “through his faith, though he died, he still speaks.” The righteous are remembered; the wicked’s memory perishes. Christ’s name is eternal: Phil 2:9–10 (“the name that is above every name”). The worm-eaten forgotten dead are the anti-type of the one who rose bodily and will never see corruption.
24.21: Exploiting the barren woman and withholding good from the widow — cruelty to the reproductively marginal. The barren who conceives by divine action is a repeated OT type: Sarah, Rebekah, Rachel, Hannah — all barren until God opens the womb. The church, described as the barren woman who now has more children than the married (Isa 54:1, quoted in Gal 4:27), is the ultimate fulfillment: those who had no covenant-status become the family of God through Christ.
24.22: God dragging the mighty by his power — the sovereign reversal of human strength. Isa 40:29–31: “he gives power to the faint, and to him who has no might he increases strength.” 1 Cor 1:27: “God chose what is weak in the world to shame the strong.” The dragging-down of the mighty is the same reversal as the Magnificat (Luke 1:52: “he has brought down the mighty from their thrones and exalted those of humble estate”).
24.23: God giving the wicked security while his eyes watch their ways — the patience of divine watching before judgment. Acts 17:30: “the times of ignorance God overlooked, but now he commands all people everywhere to repent, because he has fixed a day on which he will judge the world in righteousness by a man whom he has appointed.” The divine watching that Job observes is the watching before the appointed judgment; the appointed judge is Christ (Acts 17:31).
24.24: The wicked exalted for a little while then gone, cut down like heads of grain — the temporary nature of wickedness’ apparent triumph. Rev 20:10: “the devil who had deceived them was thrown into the lake of fire and sulfur.” The brief exaltation of the wicked — which seems permanent to those who suffer under it — is temporary in the divine perspective. The “little while” of the wicked’s triumph ends at the parousia of Christ, the one appointed to judge.
24.25: Job’s challenge: who can prove him a liar? The ultimate answer is no one, because Job correctly diagnoses both his own innocence and the empirical reality of unequal divine dealing with the righteous and wicked. In the NT, this is not resolved by argument but by eschatological promise: Rev 22:12 (“I am coming soon, bringing my recompense with me”). The recompense that Christ brings finally vindicates every Job who suffered innocently and every Eliphaz who misapplied the retributive formula.

25.1: Bildad’s third and shortest speech (6 verses) reflects the exhaustion of the friends’ theological resources. Despite its brevity, it asks the most important question in the book: how can a human being be righteous before God? In the NT this question is not dismissed but fully answered in Christ: Rom 3:21–26 (the righteousness of God through faith in Christ); 2 Cor 5:21 (“he made him to be sin who knew no sin, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God”).
25.2: God’s dominion and awesome power making peace in the heights — the sovereign peace of the divine order. Col 1:19–20: “in him all the fullness of God was pleased to dwell, and through him to reconcile to himself all things, whether on earth or in heaven, making peace by the blood of his cross.” The divine peace in the heights that Bildad describes abstractly is the peace that Christ accomplished concretely — the reconciliation of all things through the cross, including the heavenly realm.
25.3: The innumerable armies of God — the unlimited divine power. Heb 1:6: “let all God’s angels worship him” (Christ, at his entrance into the world). Matt 26:53: Jesus at his arrest: “do you think that I cannot appeal to my Father, and he will at once send me more than twelve legions of angels?” The unlimited divine armies that Bildad invokes as evidence of human smallness are the armies that stood at Christ’s disposal and which he refused to summon in order to accomplish redemption by a different means.
25.4: “How then can man be righteous before God? How can he who is born of woman be pure?” — the question the Incarnation answers. Rom 3:21–22: “the righteousness of God through faith in Jesus Christ for all who believe.” Gal 4:4: “born of woman, born under the law, to redeem those under the law, so that we might receive adoption as sons.” The “born of woman” who is pure is Christ; and those united to him receive his purity credited to them.
25.5: Even the moon is not bright and the stars are not pure in God’s eyes — the cosmic purity hierarchy. Rev 21:23: “the city has no need of sun or moon to shine on it, for the glory of God gives it light, and its lamp is the Lamb.” The moon and stars whose light falls short of divine purity will be superseded by the light of Christ himself as the lamp of the new creation. The hierarchy of brightness reaches its summit not in the stars but in the Lamb whose glory outshines them all.
25.6: “How much less man, who is a maggot, and the son of man, who is a worm!” — the nadir of human worth before divine holiness. Ps 22:6: “I am a worm and not a man” — the suffering righteous one’s self-description in the paradigmatic passion psalm. Col 1:15: “he is the image of the invisible God, the firstborn of all creation.” The worm-son-of-man is the one whom Ps 22:6 anticipates and who is identified with the divine image and firstborn of creation. The maximum creaturely humility (“worm”) and the maximum creaturely dignity (“image of God”) meet in Christ.

26.1: Job’s sarcastic response — “how you have helped him who has no power!” — demolishes the friends’ practical futility. In contrast, Christ’s help to those with no power is actual: Heb 2:18 (“because he himself has suffered when tempted, he is able to help those who are being tempted”); Heb 4:15 (“we do not have a high priest who is unable to sympathize with our weaknesses”). The help that the friends could not supply, Christ supplies by entering the weakness himself.
26.2: Job’s sarcasm continues: you have saved the arm with no strength! The irony in the NT is that Christ does exactly this — not through the superior power of human counsel but through weakness: 1 Cor 1:25 (“the weakness of God is stronger than men”); 2 Cor 12:9 (“my power is made perfect in weakness”). The salvation of the arm with no strength is the specific form divine power takes in the cross.
26.3: Counsel to the one without wisdom — the genuine counsel that the friends claimed to offer but could not supply. 1 Cor 1:30: “Christ Jesus, who has become for us wisdom from God.” Col 2:3: “in whom are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge.” The wisdom the friends lacked and could not provide, Christ is and provides: the Wisdom of God incarnate who is the answer to every wisdom question the book of Job poses.
26.4: Job challenges whose breath has spoken through Bildad — the question of the source of authoritative speech. John 16:13: “when the Spirit of truth comes, he will guide you into all the truth, for he will not speak on his own authority, but whatever he hears he will speak.” The breath of God (the Spirit) that did not speak through Bildad speaks through Christ and through the Spirit he sends; the authoritative divine voice that the friends lacked was the voice of the Spirit whom Christ would pour out.
26.5: The Rephaim trembling under the waters — even the dead dread the divine. Matt 8:29: the demons cry out to Jesus, “have you come here to torment us before the time?” — they know the appointed judge. Rev 1:18: “I died, and behold I am alive forevermore, and I have the keys of Death and Hades.” The Rephaim who tremble before God in Job 26:5 tremble before Christ as the one who has the keys of Hades.
26.6: Sheol naked before God, Abaddon uncovered — no realm outside divine visibility. Heb 4:13: “nothing in all creation is hidden from God’s sight; everything is uncovered and laid bare before the eyes of him to whom we must give account.” Rev 9:11 names Abaddon as the angel of the bottomless pit; Rev 20:14: “Death and Hades were thrown into the lake of fire.” The naked transparency of Sheol before God is the transparency before Christ who holds authority over Death and Hades.
26.7: God stretching out the north over the void, hanging the earth on nothing — the cosmic sovereignty of the Creator. Col 1:16–17: “by him all things were created, in heaven and on earth, visible and invisible, whether thrones or dominions or rulers or authorities — all things were created through him and for him. And he is before all things, and in him all things hold together.” The earth hanging on nothing is held in place by Christ’s sustaining word (Heb 1:3: “upholding the universe by the word of his power”).
26.8: The waters bound in the clouds, the cloud not splitting — the divine management of creation’s physical systems. John 1:3: “all things were made through him, and without him was not any thing made that was made.” The bound waters and the intact cloud are among the “all things” created and sustained by Christ the Word. The intricate ecological systems that Job marvels at are held together by the same one who took on flesh in the stable at Bethlehem.
26.9: God covering the face of his throne, spreading his cloud over it — the veiling of the divine throne. Heb 4:14–16: we have a great high priest who has passed through the heavens — Christ Jesus; therefore approach the throne of grace with confidence. The throne veiled in cloud that the high priest could only approach once a year through layers of ritual protection is the throne that Christ has opened permanently for those in him.
26.10: The circle inscribed on the face of the waters as the boundary between light and darkness — the divine ordering of the created world. Gen 1:3–4: God separating light from darkness was the first creative act. John 1:4–5: “in him was life, and the life was the light of men. The light shines in the darkness, and the darkness has not overcome it.” The boundary between light and darkness that God inscribed at creation is the boundary that Christ’s resurrection permanently establishes: the darkness cannot overcome the light.
26.11: The pillars of heaven trembling at God’s rebuke — the cosmic structures responding to divine authority. Matt 8:27: the disciples at the stilled storm: “what sort of man is this, that even winds and sea obey him?” The pillars of heaven that tremble at divine rebuke are the same forces that obey Christ’s voice when he rebukes the wind and sea. The sovereign authority over creation that Job’s hymn attributes to God is demonstrated in Christ’s miraculous authority over natural forces.
26.12: God stilling the sea and striking down Rahab by his understanding — the creative defeat of the chaos-monster. Rev 21:1: “then I saw a new heaven and a new earth, for the first heaven and the first earth had passed away, and the sea was no more.” The sea whose chaos-principle God struck down in Rahab is the sea that no longer exists in the new creation; the chaotic principle that threatened ordered existence is permanently defeated. Christ’s resurrection is the firstfruits of this new creation (1 Cor 15:20).
26.13: “By his breath the skies were cleared; his hand pierced the fleeing serpent” — the Spirit’s action and the defeat of the chaos-serpent. Gen 3:15: the enmity between the serpent and the woman’s seed, whose heel would be struck but who would crush the serpent’s head. Col 2:15: “he disarmed the rulers and authorities and put them to open shame, by triumphing over them in him” — the cross as the piercing of the ancient serpent. The fleeing serpent that God’s hand pierced in creation is the serpent defeated at Calvary.
26.14: “These are but the outer fringes of his works; how faint the whisper we hear of him!” — the epistemological humility before the vast incomprehensibility of God. 1 Cor 2:9: “no eye has seen, nor ear heard, nor the heart of man imagined, what God has prepared for those who love him.” 1 Cor 13:12: “now I know in part; then I shall know fully, even as I have been fully known.” The outer fringes that Job describes are the limit of present knowledge; the full knowledge awaits the face-to-face vision of Christ in the age to come.

1.1: A type: Job is introduced as 'blameless (tām) and upright (yāšār), one who feared God and turned away from evil' — the fourfold moral description establishing his character before all that follows. This description is the ground of the book's theodicy: Job suffers not because of sin but because of who he is. Christ is the ultimate blameless and upright one (Heb 7:26 'holy, blameless, undefiled, separated from sinners'), whose suffering is the definitive demonstration that righteousness does not guarantee exemption from pain.
1.2: The theme: Job's seven sons and three daughters represent the fullness of covenant blessing (the number seven as completeness). The covenant family that Job enjoys images the Father's relationship with the Son in glory — Christ possessed the full riches of divine fellowship before the Incarnation (John 17:5 'restore to me the glory I had with you before the world was created').
1.3: The theme: Job's vast possessions establish him as the greatest man of the East. The prologue is careful to note his greatness before the stripping begins, so the magnitude of the loss is legible. Christ's kenosis similarly presupposes pre-existent glory: 'though he was rich, yet for your sake he became poor' (2 Cor 8:9) — the poverty is only meaningful against the backdrop of the wealth.
1.4: The theme: the sons' feasting together with their sisters images covenantal fellowship and familial joy. The heavenly feast that Christ promises — 'I tell you, many will come from the east and the west, and will take their places at the feast with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob in the kingdom of heaven' (Matt 8:11) — is the eschatological fulfillment of this pattern of gathered family at table.
1.5: A type: Job's intercessory offering for each of his children — rising early, offering burnt offerings, saying 'perhaps my sons have sinned' — is the priestly intercession that the NT applies to Christ. Heb 7:25 'he always lives to intercede for them'; 1 John 2:1 'we have an advocate with the Father — Jesus Christ, the Righteous One.' Job intercedes as patriarch-priest; Christ intercedes as eternal high priest. The regular (kol hayyāmîm) pattern of Job's intercession prefigures the perpetual intercession of Christ.
1.6: A revelation of God: the heavenly council where the divine beings (bĕnê ʾĔlōhîm) present themselves before YHWH establishes the cosmic-legal setting for the entire book. This is the same council before which Christ ascends (Heb 9:24 'he entered heaven itself, now to appear for us in God's presence'). The drama of Job's suffering has its origin in the heavenly court; so does the drama of Christ's passion and vindication.
1.7: A revelation of God: 'the Adversary came among them... from roaming through the earth.' 1 Pet 5:8 applies this directly: 'your adversary the devil prowls around like a roaring lion seeking someone to devour.' The Adversary's roaming establishes his domain (the earth) and his method (surveillance for opportunity). Christ enters this same domain ('the Word became flesh and made his dwelling among us,' John 1:14) — the Son invades the Adversary's territory.
1.8: A type: YHWH's commendation — 'Have you taken notice of my servant Job? There is no one like him on earth' — echoes the divine commendation of the Son: 'This is my Son, whom I love; with him I am well pleased' (Matt 3:17; 17:5). Job is YHWH's servant (ʿeḇeḏ) whose unique righteousness is publicly testified by God himself. Isaiah's Servant Songs use the same ʿeḇeḏ vocabulary for the figure who will bear the sins of many (Isa 42:1; 52:13).
1.9: A revelation of God: 'Does Job fear God for no reason?' — the Adversary's challenge cuts to the heart of all human piety: is it possible to worship God disinterestedly, without expectation of reward? This is also the question about Christ's disciples: will they remain faithful without earthly benefit? The book's resolution (Job maintaining integrity after losing everything) is the OT answer; the NT answer is the cross — Christ maintains faithfulness unto death, the ultimate disinterested love.
1.10: The theme: 'Have you not put a protective fence (śĕyāg) around him?' — the divine hedge as protection. The same concept appears in Christ's prayer: John 17:11-12 'Holy Father, protect them by the power of your name... I protected them and kept them safe.' For Job, this hedge is temporarily removed for testing; for Christ's people, the hedge of intercession is permanent.
1.11: A revelation of God: 'stretch out your hand and strike everything he has — he will surely curse you to your face.' The Adversary wagers that suffering will destroy Job's faithfulness. The same wager was made about Christ at Calvary: that the extremity of suffering and abandonment would produce curse rather than trust. The cross answers both wagers: Job does not curse; Christ speaks forgiveness (Luke 23:34).
1.12: A type: 'Very well, everything he has is in your power. Only do not lay a hand on the man himself.' YHWH limits the Adversary's access to Job, permitting loss but not his person. This is the precise inverse of what happens with Christ: the Father gives the Son entirely over — Rom 8:32 'he who did not spare his own Son, but gave him up for us all.' Job's person is protected; Christ's person is surrendered. The comparison illuminates the greater depth of Christ's sacrifice.
1.13: The theme: the day of feasting when disaster strikes — the enjoyment of covenant blessing at the moment of its catastrophic removal. The Last Supper has this quality: the night of fellowship and covenant when the betrayal, arrest, and passion begin (Matt 26:20-30). The feast before the stripping.
1.14: The theme: the first messenger's report — the oxen and donkeys seized. The sequential stripping of Job's possessions by successive reports is the prologue's technique of narrating total loss. The progressive denudation mirrors the Passion sequence, where Jesus is stripped of clothing (John 19:23-24), dignity (the mocking, Matt 27:28-31), and life.
1.15: The theme: 'I alone escaped to tell you' — the sole survivor formula repeated four times. The solitary witness who escapes to report bears the weight of the news alone. Christ's disciples fled (Mark 14:50 'everyone deserted him and fled'); the women at the tomb became solitary witnesses to the resurrection (Mark 16:1-8; Luke 24:1-10).
1.16: The theme: 'fire from God (ʾĕš ʾĔlōhîm) fell from heaven' — the second disaster invokes divine agency explicitly: not merely human enemies but apparently divine fire. Job experiences divine action as catastrophic. The NT reframes divine fire: Heb 12:29 'our God is a consuming fire' — but for those in Christ, this same fire is purifying rather than destroying (1 Pet 1:7).
1.17: The theme: the Chaldeans take the camels in the third disaster. The pattern of multi-front assault — enemies from multiple directions — prefigures the Passion's multi-front attack on Christ: Judas betraying, priests accusing, Roman soldiers executing, crowds mocking. Every human agent participates in the stripping.
1.18: The theme: the fourth and final disaster strikes the children themselves — the dearest possessions last. The pattern of progressive loss until the most precious is taken is also the Passion's movement: health taken, reputation taken, disciples taken, life taken. 'He who did not spare his own Son' (Rom 8:32) — the ultimate non-sparing.
1.19: The theme: 'a great wind (rûaḥ gĕḏôlāh) came out of the wilderness and struck the four corners of the house.' The wind that destroys Job's household uses the same vocabulary (rûaḥ = wind/spirit; gāḏôl = great) as the divine power. The wilderness origin of the destroying wind connects to the wilderness where Jesus was tempted (Matt 4:1-11) and where the Adversary operates.
1.20: A type: Job's threefold mourning response — tearing his garment, shaving his head, falling to the ground in worship — is the ancient mourning liturgy (Gen 37:34 Jacob; 2 Sam 1:11 David). The fall to the ground in worship distinguishes Job's response from mere lamentation: grief and trust together. Christ's Gethsemane parallels this — 'he fell with his face to the ground and prayed' (Matt 26:39), the physical prostration of utter surrender.
1.21: A type: 'Naked I came from my mother's womb, and naked I will return. The LORD gave, and the LORD has taken away; may the name of the LORD be praised.' This is the theological high point of the prologue — the archetypal response to total loss. Paul develops the same logic: Phil 4:11-12 'I have learned to be content whatever the circumstances'; 1 Tim 6:7 'we brought nothing into the world, and we can take nothing out of it.' Christ's kenosis is the ultimate expression of this naked coming and returning: he empties himself (Phil 2:7) to become poor (2 Cor 8:9) so that we might be clothed (Rev 3:18).
1.22: A type: 'Job did not sin or accuse God of wrongdoing (tiplāh = what is unseemly/wrong)' — the blameless response to catastrophe. 1 Pet 2:22-23 applies exactly this standard to Christ: 'he committed no sin, and no deceit was found in his mouth. When they hurled their insults at him, he did not retaliate; when he suffered, he made no threats. Instead, he entrusted himself to him who judges justly.' Job anticipates the pattern; Christ perfects it.

2.1: A revelation of God: the second assembly of the heavenly council with the Adversary echoes the first (1:6) but with an intensified charge. The repetition of the cosmic-legal setting establishes that Job's suffering is not random or impersonal but part of an ongoing forensic process in the divine court. Christ's trial before Pilate has this same character: a human court reflecting a cosmic legal reality.
2.2: The theme: 'from roaming through the earth and walking back and forth on it' — repeated from 1:7. The Adversary's continued surveillance of the earth is the ongoing backdrop of human suffering. 1 Pet 5:8 'be sober-minded; be watchful. Your adversary the devil prowls around like a roaring lion, seeking someone to devour' — the danger is real and continuous; the defense is Christ's intercession (Heb 7:25).
2.3: A type: 'He still maintains his integrity, even though you incited me against him to ruin him without cause (ḥinnām).' The phrase 'without cause' is the Septuagint's ἐμάτην — and Ps 35:19 / 69:4 use the same word for the enemies who hate the psalmist 'without cause.' John 15:25 applies Ps 35:19 to Christ explicitly: 'they hated me without reason/without cause.' Job is ruined without cause; Christ is hated without cause — both the unjust sufferer whose suffering has redemptive power.
2.4: A revelation of God: 'Skin for skin! A man will give everything he has to save his own life.' The Adversary's cynical anthropology — that human piety is purely self-interested — is the doctrine of human selfishness the Incarnation and the cross directly refute. John 15:13 'greater love has no one than this: to lay down one's life for one's friends.' Mark 10:45 'the Son of Man did not come to be served, but to serve, and to give his life as a ransom for many.' Christ gives his life rather than preserving it — the complete inversion of the Adversary's claim.
2.5: A type: 'reach out now and strike his flesh and his bones' — the Adversary's request to strike the body of the innocent man is granted. This is Passion language: John 19:1 (the flogging); 19:17-18 (the crucifixion); 19:31-34 (the breaking of legs and the piercing). Christ's flesh and bones are struck so that his body becomes the site of redemption — the struck body of the scapegoat that carries away sin (Heb 13:12).
2.6: A direct contrast: 'Very well, he is in your hands. Only spare his life.' YHWH permits total bodily suffering but withholds Job's life. This is the inverse of the cross: for the Son, God does NOT spare — Rom 8:32 'he who did not spare (οὐκ ἐφείσατο) his own Son but gave him up for us all.' The contrast is deliberate: Job's life is spared so that the theological question can continue; Christ's life is not spared so that the redemptive answer can be given.
2.7: A type: 'The Adversary afflicted Job with painful, festering sores from the soles of his feet to the top of his head.' The top-to-bottom totality of the suffering echoes Isa 1:6 ('from the sole of the foot to the crown of the head there is no soundness, only wounds and welts and open sores'). Isaiah uses this as a description of Israel's covenant devastation; the same totality applies to the Passion's physical suffering (crown of thorns to nailed feet). Total suffering for the innocent one.
2.8: The theme: Job taking a piece of pottery to scrape his sores while sitting among the ashes — the image of the greatest man of the East reduced to ash-heap and broken pottery. Lam 3:16 'he has broken my teeth with gravel; he has trampled me in the dust.' Christ takes on this ultimate lowliness — Phil 2:8 'he humbled himself by becoming obedient to death — even death on a cross'; the ash-heap as the furthest descent before the exaltation (Phil 2:9).
2.9: A type: Job's wife says 'Are you still holding on to your integrity? Curse God and be done with it' — a temptation to abandon covenant faithfulness through death. The temptations of the cross mock Christ in the same way: Matt 27:40 'come down from the cross, if you are the Son of God!'; Luke 23:37 'save yourself if you are the King of the Jews!' Both Job and Christ are urged to escape suffering by abandoning their covenant relationship with God; both refuse.
2.10: A type: 'Shall we accept good from God and refuse to accept adversity?' — the theological question that distills the book's challenge. Christ's Gethsemane prayer is the perfect expression of this acceptance: 'My Father, if it is possible, may this cup be taken from me. Yet not as I will, but as you will' (Matt 26:39). The cup of adversity accepted rather than refused is the mechanism of redemption. 'Job did not sin with his lips' — the parallel with 1 Pet 2:22 ('no deceit was found in his mouth') is exact.
2.11: The theme: the three friends Eliphaz, Bildad, and Zophar come from distant regions to 'express their sympathy and comfort him' (lĕnûḏ lô wĕlĕnaḥămô). The Hebrew nāḥam (comfort) is the root of both the Comfort/consolation of Israel that Simeon waited for (Luke 2:25) and the Paraclete (ΠαράκλητοϚ = the one called alongside to help/comfort, John 14:16). The friends fail as comforters; Christ and his Spirit succeed.
2.12: A type: 'When they looked up from a distance, they could not even recognize him.' The radical disfigurement that makes Job unrecognizable is the same vocabulary as Isa 52:14: 'his appearance was so disfigured beyond that of any human being, and his form marred beyond human likeness.' The unrecognizable sufferer is the Servant whose disfigurement is simultaneous with his atoning work.
2.13: A revelation of God: 'They sat on the ground beside him for seven days and seven nights. No one spoke a single word to him, for they could see how overwhelming his suffering was.' The seven-day silent presence is the appropriate response to acute grief (the friends' later failure is speaking, not silence). Christ's presence with the grieving is similarly one of incarnate solidarity — John 11:35 'Jesus wept'; he sits with the grieving rather than immediately resolving the grief.

3.1: A revelation of God: 'After this, Job opened his mouth and cursed the day he was born.' The move from silent suffering to voiced despair marks the beginning of the poetic dialogue. Job's curse of his own birth represents the maximum darkness of fallen human experience — the wish for non-existence. Christ does not curse the day of his birth (John 18:37 'for this reason I was born'), but he enters into the reality of those who do (Heb 4:15 'tempted in every way, just as we are').
3.2: The theme: 'Job spoke and said' — the opening of his first lamentation speech. The literary device of the wisdom dialogue begins here; Job's speeches will become progressively more theologically daring until the divine answer. Christ's own cry from the cross (Matt 27:46 'my God, my God, why have you forsaken me?') is the apex of innocent lament taken up by God himself.
3.3: The theme: 'Let the day on which I was born perish, and the night that announced, a boy has been conceived.' The opposite of the Annunciation — where Gabriel announces a birth with 'you will conceive and give birth to a son' (Luke 1:31), and heaven celebrates (Luke 2:10-14). Job wishes away the announcement of his birth; the angelic announcement of Christ's birth is the proclamation of humanity's rescue.
3.4: The theme: 'Let that day be turned to darkness. May God above not care for it, nor light shine upon it.' The theological inverse of Gen 1:3 ('God said, let there be light'). Job wishes the re-primordial darkness of uncreation upon the day of his birth. The NT's eschatological promise is the final reversal of this darkness: Rev 21:23 'the city does not need the sun or the moon... for the glory of God gives it light, and the Lamb is its lamp.'
3.5: The theme: 'May darkness and deep shadow (ṣalmāwet) reclaim it.' ṣalmāwet (shadow of death/deep darkness) is Job's signature darkness-term (3:5; 10:21-22; 12:22; 16:16; 24:17; 28:3; 34:22; 38:17). The NT applies this darkness to the condition before Christ: Luke 1:79 'to shine on those living in darkness and in the shadow of death (σκιᾷ θανάτου)' — quoting Isa 9:2, the Messianic promise. Christ is the light that pierces ṣalmāwet.
3.6: The theme: 'Let pitch darkness take it; let it not be counted among the days of the year.' The wish for erasure from the calendar of time. The NT vision of time's redemption goes in the opposite direction: every day is redeemed by Christ's presence (Eph 5:16 'making the most of every opportunity, because the days are evil'). Time itself is reclaimed rather than cursed.
3.7: The theme: 'Let that night be barren; let no sound of joy enter it.' The barren night without joy is the antithesis of the song of the angels at Christ's birth (Luke 2:14 'glory to God in the highest heaven, and on earth peace to those on whom his favor rests'). Job's darkness-wish is inverted by the Incarnation's light-proclamation.
3.8: A shadow: 'Let those who are skilled at cursing days put a curse on it — those who know how to rouse Leviathan.' Leviathan (lĕwiyāṯān) appears throughout Job (3:8; 41:1) as the chaos monster. Rev 12:9 and 20:2 identify the great dragon/serpent (ὁ δράκων ὁ μέγας) as the ancient serpent, the devil. Christ's conquest of the dragon/Leviathan is the eschatological answer to Job's invocation of the chaos-cursers.
3.9: The theme: 'May it wait for light that never comes; may it never see the breaking of morning.' The desperate non-dawn anticipates John 1:5 ('the light shines in the darkness') and 8:12 ('I am the light of the world; whoever follows me will never walk in darkness but will have the light of life'). The morning that never comes for Job's cursed day does come in Christ — the resurrection morning that breaks the permanent darkness.
3.10: The theme: 'Because it did not close the doors of my mother's womb to me, nor hide trouble from my eyes.' Job wishes he had never seen — the ultimate recoil from experience. The NT does not promise the avoidance of trouble but transformation through it: John 16:33 'in this world you will have trouble. But take heart! I have overcome the world.' Christ has seen the trouble and overcome it.
3.11: The theme: 'Why did I not die at birth?' — Job's first death-wish, the wish for non-existence over existence-with-suffering. Heb 2:9 'he tasted death for everyone' — Christ takes on what Job wishes had happened to him, turning death from a wish for escape into an accomplished redemption.
3.12: The theme: 'Why were there knees to receive me, and breasts for me to nurse?' — the received-and-nurtured infant as an image of unwanted gift. Luke 2:22-24 presents the infant Christ at the Temple, received with Simeon's arms (v28) — the welcoming reception of the child who comes as God's gift to the world, the anti-type of Job's unwanted birth.
3.13: The theme: 'For then I would have lain down and been at rest' — the rest of non-existence that Job desires. The NT redirects this longing: Heb 4:9-10 'there remains a Sabbath-rest for the people of God; for anyone who enters God's rest also rests from their works, just as God did from his.' The true rest is not in non-existence but in the completed work of Christ.
3.14: The theme: 'with kings and counselors of the earth who built themselves ruins' — the shared rest of the great with the forgotten. The leveling of status in death is the OT's recognition of human equality before mortality. James 2:1-4 applies the same leveling to Christ's assembly; Rev 7:9-10 shows the great multitude of every nation, tribe, and tongue equally before the throne.
3.15: The theme: 'or with princes who hoarded gold and filled their mansions with silver.' The futility of accumulated wealth when death is the leveler. Matt 6:19-21 ('do not store up for yourselves treasures on earth') and Luke 12:16-21 (the rich fool) apply this OT wisdom. Christ alone offers treasure that death cannot level (Matt 6:20 'store up for yourselves treasures in heaven').
3.16: The shadow: 'Or why was I not like a stillborn hidden away, like infants who never saw the light of day?' The stillborn hidden away — the untimely death that never saw light — anticipates the NT's use of premature death as a metaphor for being dead in sin before Christ: Eph 2:1-5 'as for you, you were dead in your transgressions and sins... God... made us alive with Christ.' The spiritual stillborn who sees no light is given life through Christ.
3.17: A revelation of God: 'There the wicked cease from their turmoil, and there the exhausted find rest.' The vision of Sheol as rest from labor. Rev 14:13 appropriates exactly this: 'blessed are the dead who die in the Lord from now on... they will rest from their labor, for their deeds will follow them.' The rest that Job imagines in death is redeemed in Christ into resurrection rest.
3.18: The theme: 'There the prisoners rest together; they no longer hear the taskmaster's shout.' The end of oppression in death. Christ proclaims release to prisoners (Luke 4:18, quoting Isa 61:1) and breaks the power of the last taskmaster — death — through his resurrection. The rest that Job imagines is real in Christ, but it comes through resurrection, not dissolution.
3.19: The theme: 'The lowly and the great are alike there, and the slave is free from his master.' Death's leveling is the OT observation; the NT reframes it through resurrection: Gal 3:28 'there is neither Jew nor Gentile, neither slave nor free, nor is there male and female, for you are all one in Christ Jesus.' The equality that death produces by ending everything is surpassed by the equality Christ creates by elevating everything.
3.20: A revelation of God: 'Why is light given to the one who suffers, and life to those whose souls are bitter?' The theodicy question in its starkest form — why does God sustain life when life means suffering? John 1:4 'in him was life, and that life was the light of all mankind' — the NT answer is that God gives light through the person of Christ, not as an abstract gift but as an incarnate presence who shares the suffering light is given into.
3.21: The theme: 'those who long for death that does not come, who search for it more eagerly than for buried treasure.' The desperate longing for death that remains unfulfilled is the condition of deep spiritual desolation. The NT response is 1 Cor 15:55 'where, O death, is your victory? Where, O death, is your sting?' — the sting of death (the fear that makes it desirable as escape) is removed by Christ's resurrection, transforming death from the desired exit into the defeated enemy.
3.22: The theme: 'who would be overjoyed and exult when they finally reach the grave.' The perverse joy at finding death is the maximum inversion of the gospel's invitation. Phil 4:4 'rejoice in the Lord always; I will say it again: rejoice!' — the NT places joy not in reaching the grave but in belonging to the risen Christ who has conquered the grave.
3.23: A type: 'Why is life given to a man whose way is hidden, whom God has fenced in (sāḵaḵ) on every side?' Job uses the Adversary's own vocabulary (the śĕyāg/hedge of 1:10) now as complaint: the divine encirclement experienced as imprisonment rather than protection. Christ is 'fenced in' to human limitation by the Incarnation (Gal 4:4 'born of woman, born under the law') — the omnipotent Son enclosed in the restrictions of mortality for redemptive purpose.
3.24: The theme: 'my groaning comes even before my food' — grief displacing appetite. The Psalms of lament use the same image: Ps 102:4-5 'I forget to eat my food... I am reduced to skin and bones.' Christ identifies with those who grieve and fast: Matt 6:16-18 affirms fasting as a genuine spiritual discipline; the Gethsemane night is itself an extended experience of grief overwhelming the body.
3.25: A revelation of God: 'What I dreaded most has befallen me; what I feared has come upon me.' The realization of the worst fear is the human experience that Christ enters fully. Heb 5:7 'during the days of Jesus's life on earth, he offered up prayers and petitions with fervent cries and tears to the one who could save him from death, and he was heard because of his reverent submission.' The worst fear — abandonment in death — is precisely what Christ faces at Calvary (Matt 27:46).
3.26: The theme: 'I had no ease, no rest, no peace — yet trouble still came.' The cumulative deprivation — no ease (šalwāh), no rest (šāqaṭ), no peace (šāqaṭ) — describes the pre-gospel human condition without divine peace. The NT reverses this threefold absence: John 14:27 'Peace I leave with you; my peace I give you'; Phil 4:7 'the peace of God, which transcends all understanding, will guard your hearts and minds in Christ Jesus.'

4.1: The theme: Eliphaz the Temanite's first speech begins the friends' failed pastoral engagement. Eliphaz represents wise human theology in its most confident form — and his failure to help Job (condemned by God in ch42:7) demonstrates that even excellent human wisdom is insufficient for the theodicy Job faces. The knowledge of God in Christ (Col 2:3) is what surpasses Eliphaz's wisdom.
4.2: The theme: 'If someone ventures a word with you, will it trouble you?' Eliphaz asks permission before speaking — a pastoral sensitivity his later speeches lose. The NT wisdom: Jas 1:19 'everyone should be quick to listen, slow to speak and slow to become angry.' The ministry of presence before the ministry of words is what the friends provide briefly (2:13) and then abandon.
4.3: A type: 'Look — you have instructed many people, and you have strengthened (ḥāzaq) weak hands.' The strengthening of weak hands is the language of Isa 35:3-4: 'Strengthen (ḥazzĕqû) the feeble hands, steady the knees that give way; say to those with fearful hearts: Be strong, do not fear; your God will come.' Isaiah applies this to the Messianic age of restoration. Eliphaz sees Job as having performed this Messianic ministry; now the question is whether Job can receive what he has given.
4.4: A type: 'Your words have upheld the one who was stumbling, and you have steadied feeble knees.' The pastoral ministry described here is precisely the Messianic ministry of Christ: Matt 12:20 'a bruised reed he will not break, and a smoldering wick he will not snuff out' (quoting Isa 42:3); Heb 12:12 'strengthen your feeble arms and weak knees' (quoting Isa 35:3). Eliphaz inadvertently describes what Christ will permanently accomplish — the steadying of those who stumble.
4.5: The theme: 'But now that it has come upon you, you are impatient; it has touched you, and you are terrified.' Eliphaz diagnoses the gap between Job's pastoral wisdom-for-others and his failure to apply it to himself. The NT addresses this same gap: the servant who forgives others but demands repayment from his fellow servant (Matt 18:28-30); and Christ himself who models what he teaches — bearing suffering without impatience (1 Pet 2:23).
4.6: A revelation of God: 'Is not your reverence for God your confidence, and the integrity of your ways your hope?' Eliphaz identifies two foundations — fear of God and integrity. The NT grounds both in Christ: Eph 3:12 'in him and through faith in him we may approach God with freedom and confidence'; Heb 10:22 'let us draw near to God with a sincere heart and with the full assurance that faith brings.' Confidence before God is not earned by integrity but given through Christ.
4.7: A direct challenge: 'Think: has any innocent person ever perished? Have the upright ever been destroyed?' Eliphaz assumes the answer is no. The answer given at Calvary is: one innocent person did perish — the only truly upright person was destroyed. And precisely because of his innocence, his destruction saves. 1 Pet 3:18 'Christ also suffered once for sins, the righteous for the unrighteous, to bring you to God.'
4.8: The theme: 'those who plow trouble and sow wickedness reap the same' — the retributive principle that is generally true. Gal 6:7-8 'a man reaps what he sows: the one who sows to please his sinful nature, from that nature will reap destruction.' But Christ reaps what others sow: 2 Cor 5:21 'God made him who had no sin to be sin for us, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God.' The retributive principle is absorbed and reversed at the cross.
4.9: A shadow: 'By the breath of God (nišmaṯ-ʾēl) they perish, and by the blast of his nostrils they are consumed.' The divine breath as instrument of judgment. Isa 11:4 'he will strike the earth with the rod of his mouth; with the breath of his lips he will slay the wicked' — applied to the Messiah. 2 Thess 2:8 'the Lord Jesus will overthrow [the lawless one] with the breath of his mouth and destroy him by the splendor of his coming.' The divine breath of judgment becomes the Messianic breath.
4.10: The shadow: 'The roar of the lion, the voice of the fierce lion — the teeth of the young lions are shattered.' The silenced lions image divine judgment on the proud and strong. Christ is the 'Lion of the tribe of Judah' (Rev 5:5) who conquers not through roaring but through being slain — 'a Lamb, looking as if it had been slain.' The true Lion wins by being the sacrificial Lamb; the false lions' teeth are shattered.
4.11: The theme: 'The mature lion perishes for lack of prey, and the cubs of the lioness scatter.' The futility of predatory power — the apex predator ultimately starving. The Adversary who roams the earth seeking to devour (1 Pet 5:8) will ultimately be cast into the lake of fire (Rev 20:10) — the predator's final perishing. Christ's resurrection is the beginning of the Adversary's starvation.
4.12: A revelation of God: 'A word came to me in secret (gānaḇ = stolen secretly), and my ear caught a faint whisper of it.' The revelation that comes as a whisper in the night is the mode of divine communication before the prophetic word. The NT culmination: Heb 1:1-2 'in the past God spoke to our ancestors through the prophets at many times and in various ways, but in these last days he has spoken to us by his Son.' The whispered word gives way to the incarnate Word.
4.13: A revelation of God: 'In disturbing thoughts from visions of the night, when deep sleep descends on people' — the night vision as the vehicle of divine revelation. This is the form of divine communication in the Genesis patriarchal narratives (Gen 15:12-17 Abram's deep sleep/covenant; 28:10-17 Jacob's ladder). Joseph receives dreams (Gen 37:5-11); Pharaoh too (Gen 41). In the NT, Joseph (Jesus's father) receives guidance through night visions (Matt 1:20; 2:13), keeping the pattern while fulfilling it.
4.14: A revelation of God: 'Fear came over me and trembling, making all my bones shake.' The physical response to divine approach — trembling and shaking — is the standard theophany response: Dan 10:7-9 (Daniel falls facedown); Rev 1:17 (John falls at Jesus's feet 'as if dead') when the glorified Christ appears. The body's response to divine presence is universal: awe that is also terror until the divine word assures.
4.15: A revelation of God: 'A spirit passed before my face; the hair on my body bristled.' The passing spirit echoes Exod 33:22-23 (Moses hidden in the cleft of the rock while YHWH's glory passes) and 1 Kings 19:11-13 (Elijah experiencing the passing of YHWH in the still small voice). The NT equivalent is the post-resurrection appearances where Christ passes through walls (John 20:19) and is suddenly present — the recognizable-yet-different quality of the glorified Christ.
4.16: A revelation of God: 'It stopped still, but I could not make out its form. A shape was before my eyes; silence — and then a voice.' The unrecognizable form that speaks is the primary mode of theophany: Exod 24:17 (divine appearance as consuming fire); Isa 6:1-5 (the LORD on his throne — the form not fully visible); Ezek 1:26-28 (the figure on the throne, described by analogy, never directly). John 1:18 'no one has ever seen God, but the one and only Son, who is himself God and is in closest relationship with the Father, has made him known.'
4.17: A direct question: 'Can a mortal be more righteous than God? Can a person be purer than their Maker?' Eliphaz poses this as a rhetorical negative. The Incarnation complicates the answer: in Christ, God takes on mortal form — the Maker enters the clay house of creation (John 1:14). The question is not about comparing creature to Creator but about whether the Creator can become creature — which he does. 2 Cor 5:21 'God made him who had no sin to be sin for us, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God.'
4.18: A revelation of God: 'God places no trust even in his servants; he charges his very angels with error.' The angelic imperfection before God establishes that the mediator cannot be an angel. Heb 1:5-14 explicitly argues Christ's superiority to the angels — he is not a servant (v14, angels are 'ministering spirits') but a Son (v5, 'to which of the angels did God ever say, you are my Son'?). The Mediator needed cannot come from the angelic order.
4.19: A shadow: 'How much more those who live in houses of clay (bāttê-ḥōmer), whose foundation is the dust, who are crushed more easily than a moth!' The clay house (ḥōmer = clay, the material of Gen 2:7's creation) is the human body as mortal dwelling. 2 Cor 5:1 uses this exact imagery: 'if the earthly tent we live in is destroyed, we have a building from God, an eternal house in heaven, not built by human hands.' Paul's 'tent' and Eliphaz's 'clay house' are the same image; the NT adds the promise of the resurrection body.
4.20: A revelation of God: 'Between morning and evening they are destroyed; without anyone noticing, they vanish forever.' The brevity and unnoticed disappearance of human life. Christ enters this brevity (Gal 4:4 'born of woman, born under the law') and transforms it: John 11:25-26 'I am the resurrection and the life. The one who believes in me will live, even though they die; and whoever lives by believing in me will never die.' The vanishing is answered by the resurrection.
4.21: A shadow: 'Is not their tent stake pulled up from within them? They die, and that without gaining wisdom.' The tent whose stake is pulled up — the body at death — is 2 Cor 5:1-4's imagery: 'we know that if the earthly tent we live in is destroyed, we have a building from God... Meanwhile we groan, longing to be clothed instead with our heavenly dwelling.' Paul develops the tent-death-building-resurrection movement that Eliphaz's metaphor anticipates. The dying-without-wisdom is exactly what Christ reverses: those in him die having gained the Wisdom of God (1 Cor 1:30).

30.1: A shadow: Job's social inversion — those of lower social standing now mock the man of honor. The NT's deepest statement of this pattern is the Passion: the Son of God is mocked by soldiers, bystanders, and crucified criminals (Matt 27:29-44). The honor-reversal Job experiences is the structural shape of Christ's humiliation; the resurrection is the re-inversion.
30.2: A theme: the dismissal of the useless — those whose vitality has perished. The NT's transformation is the inclusion of the weak: 'God chose what is weak in the world to shame the strong' (1 Cor 1:27). The very people whom the world has written off as past their vigor are the subjects of God's particular attention.
30.3: A theme: want and famine — the outcasts who survive on the margins. The NT's beatitude addresses this: 'Blessed are those who hunger and thirst for righteousness, for they shall be satisfied' (Matt 5:6). The ones driven to barren places by deprivation are the same ones Christ declares blessed.
30.4: A theme: saltbush and broom root — the desperate diet of the marginalized. The NT shows Christ eating with the outcast (Luke 15:2), feeding the hungry in desolate places (Mark 6:30-44), and being sustained by his father's will 'as food' (John 4:34). The desolate place is where Christ regularly appears.
30.5: A revelation of God: driven out from society, shouted after like thieves — the social death of the ostracized. Christ himself was 'despised and rejected by men' (Isa 53:3) and crucified outside the city gate (Heb 13:12-13). The letter to the Hebrews draws this out as the pattern for disciples: 'let us go to him outside the camp, bearing the reproach he endured.'
30.6: A theme: living in gullies, holes, and rocks — the dispossessed dwelling in the earth's margins. The NT's parallel: 'foxes have holes and birds of the air have nests, but the Son of Man has nowhere to lay his head' (Matt 8:20). The one who made the earth had no fixed home in it.
30.7: A theme: crying out among the bushes — the inarticulate lament of the destitute. The NT holds that the Spirit intercedes for us 'with groanings too deep for words' (Rom 8:26) — the Spirit takes up the voiceless cry of the suffering and brings it before God. The cry beneath the nettles is heard.
30.8: A theme: 'children of fools, children of men without a name' — those who carry no honorable lineage. The NT's reversal: 'to all who did receive him, who believed in his name, he gave the right to become children of God' (John 1:12). The nameless are given the name; those without lineage are adopted into the family of God.
30.9: A type: 'I have become their song and their byword' — the mocked one whose name is a taunt. Psalm 69:12, which the NT applies to Christ (John 15:25, Rom 15:3), says the same: 'those who sit in the gate talk about me, and the drunkards make songs about me.' Job's becoming the occasion of mockery is the same pattern the NT sees fulfilled in Christ's passion.
30.10: A type: 'they despise me and keep their distance; they do not hesitate to spit in my face' — the full sequence of social rejection and physical contempt. The NT fulfillment is explicit: the soldiers 'spit on him' (Matt 27:30); he was 'despised and rejected' (Isa 53:3). Job's suffering here carries the structural shape of the Passion's humiliation sequence.
30.11: A revelation of God: 'God has loosed my cord and afflicted me' — Job attributes his social collapse to divine action. The NT's parallel is the Passion: 'this Jesus, delivered up according to the definite plan and foreknowledge of God, you crucified and killed by the hands of lawless men' (Acts 2:23). Divine intent and human wickedness operate simultaneously in both cases.
30.12: A shadow: 'the mob rises up; they push aside my feet and build their roads of ruin against me' — the organized assault of enemies. The NT's image of the crowd pressing against Christ and the systematic legal apparatus that condemned him has this same character of organized, coordinated assault.
30.13: A shadow: 'they need no one to help them' — the self-sustaining momentum of hostility against the righteous. The opposition to Christ is similarly depicted as needing no external motivation: the chief priests, Pilate, the crowd each act from their own interests, yet together they accomplish what the NT calls the Father's eternal purpose (Acts 4:27-28).
30.14: A shadow: 'they rush in amid the ruin' — enemies pouring through the breach like floodwater. The Passion has this quality: once the arrest began, the cascade of betrayal, denial, trial, and crucifixion followed with a logic that no human power arrested. The disciples fled; the crowd demanded death.
30.15: A shadow: 'terrors are unleashed against me; my dignity is driven away like the wind, my prosperity like a cloud' — the stripping of all exterior honor. The NT's Passion strips Christ of every dignity: garments divided, crown of thorns, the charge of blasphemy, death between criminals. What Job describes as social and economic stripping, Christ undergoes completely.
30.16: A shadow: 'my soul is poured out within me' — the language of total interior depletion. Isaiah 53:12 uses the same 'pour out' language of Christ: 'he poured out his soul to death.' The interior desolation Job describes — not just physical but the inner pouring out of self — is the pattern the Servant's death embodies completely.
30.17: A revelation of God: 'my bones are racked with pain, and the pain that gnaws at me never rests' — the unrelenting physical suffering. The NT identifies Christ as the one 'acquainted with grief' (Isa 53:3) and who 'suffered in the flesh' (1 Pet 4:1). The incarnation means God knows what it is to have bones that ache.
30.18: A shadow: 'with great force my clothing is seized by my disease; it binds me at the neck' — disease that grasps and restricts. At the crucifixion, Christ's garments are taken from him by force (Matt 27:35) — the reversal of clothing as bondage into clothing as plunder.
30.19: A type: 'he has thrown me into the mud; I have become like dust and ashes' — total reduction to the earth from which humanity was made (Gen 2:7). The NT's descent language applies this to Christ: 'he was buried' (1 Cor 15:4) — returned to the earth before being raised from it. Job's mud is the grave's anticipation.
30.20: A type: 'I cry to you but you do not answer; I stand, and you only stare at me' — the unanswered cry to God. The direct NT parallel is Psalm 22:2 ('I cry by day, but you do not answer'), which Matthew 27:46 applies to the crucifixion. Job's experience of divine silence is the paradigmatic expression of the Passion's most terrifying moment.
30.21: A shadow: 'you have become cruel to me; with the power of your hand you oppose me' — God as the apparent adversary. At the cross, God 'did not spare his own Son but gave him up for us all' (Rom 8:32) — the divine hand that opposed the Son did so on behalf of those who deserve opposition. The apparent cruelty is the mechanism of the deepest love.
30.22: A revelation of God: 'you lift me up on the wind; you cause me to ride it and dissolve my substance' — being borne away by divine power toward dissolution. The NT speaks of Christ being 'lifted up' (John 3:14, 12:32) — the crucifixion and the ascension both use this language. The lifting that Job experiences as being dissolved, Christ transforms into the lifting that draws all people to himself.
30.23: A shadow: 'I know you will bring me to death, to the house appointed for all the living' — the certainty of death. Christ goes to the same house: he 'tasted death for everyone' (Heb 2:9), entering the house appointed for all the living so that those in him might exit it by resurrection. The certainty Job accepts becomes the certainty Christ transforms.
30.24: A revelation of God: 'does not one in a heap of ruins reach out his hand? does one not cry out in his disaster?' — the natural reflex of the suffering to call for help. The NT's response is that God has provided exactly this: 'for everyone who calls on the name of the Lord will be saved' (Rom 10:13). The cry from the heap of ruins is the prayer God answers.
30.25: A revelation of God: 'did I not weep for those in trouble? was not my soul grieved for the poor?' — Job's compassionate identification with the suffering. The NT reveals this as the divine character embodied in Christ: 'Jesus wept' (John 11:35) over Lazarus; he had 'compassion on them' (Matt 9:36) for the crowds. The empathy Job describes is the empathy of the incarnate God.
30.26: A revelation of God: 'when I looked for good, evil came; when I waited for light, darkness came' — the reversal of expectation in suffering. The disciples experienced this at Golgotha: they waited for the kingdom and got a cross. Easter is the reversal: the darkness of Friday becomes the light of Sunday, the evil absorbed becomes the good released.
30.27: A theme: 'my inner parts churn and do not rest' — the churning of unresolved suffering. The NT holds that present suffering produces endurance (Rom 5:3-4), not because the churning ceases but because it is working toward something. The restlessness is purposeful.
30.28: A shadow: 'I walk in mourning without the sun; I stand up in the assembly and cry out' — public mourning without light. The NT parallel: at the crucifixion, 'from the sixth hour there was darkness over all the land until the ninth hour' (Matt 27:45), and Christ cries out from within it. The assembly's response to the righteous sufferer is silence or mockery; God's response is resurrection.
30.29: A shadow: 'I have become a brother to jackals and a companion to wild birds' — identification with wild creatures of desolation. The NT places Christ in similar wilderness company: 'he was in the wilderness forty days... and he was with the wild animals' (Mark 1:13). The tamed world that should receive the righteous has expelled them to the wild margins.
30.30: A revelation of God: 'my skin has turned black; my bones burn with fever' — the physical signs of disease and dying. The incarnation is the Word taking on exactly this burning, blackening, mortal flesh. 'He himself bore our griefs and carried our sorrows' (Isa 53:4) — the specific physiology of suffering is what God bore in the Son.
30.31: A shadow: 'my harp has been turned to mourning and my flute to the sound of weeping' — the transformation of musical joy into lament. The NT holds that this mourning has an eschatological reversal: 'you will be sorrowful, but your sorrow will turn into joy' (John 16:20). The harp restored is the NT's image of the new creation's worship (Rev 5:8, 14:2).

31.1: A shadow: 'I made a covenant with my eyes; how then could I look upon a young woman?' — sexual purity as covenantal integrity. Christ radicalized this: 'everyone who looks at a woman with lustful intent has already committed adultery with her in his heart' (Matt 5:28). Job's covenant-with-eyes is the OT form of the heart-purity Christ requires and himself perfectly embodies.
31.2: A revelation of God: the 'portion God assigns from above' — the divine allocation of fate and reward. The NT grounds this in Christ: 'in him we have obtained an inheritance' (Eph 1:11), determined by divine purpose. The portion that comes 'from above' is secured through the one who himself came 'from above' (John 3:31).
31.3: A revelation of God: 'is there not ruin for the unrighteous?' — the retribution principle that underlies Job's oath. The NT confirms the principle while transforming its application: the ruin deserved by the unrighteous fell on Christ, 'the righteous for the unrighteous, that he might bring us to God' (1 Pet 3:18). The principle stands; the one who bore it is unexpected.
31.4: A revelation of God: 'does he not see my ways and number all my steps?' — the divine omniscience as the ground of Job's oath. The NT confirms and personalizes this: 'even the hairs of your head are all numbered' (Matt 10:30). The same all-seeing God before whom Job stands is the Father who knows every detail of our lives.
31.5: A theme: 'if I have walked in falsehood, or my foot has rushed toward deceit' — the moral audit of one's path. Christ is 'the way' (John 14:6) — not just the destination but the pattern of walking. The one who invites disciples to walk in his way is himself the one who never rushed toward deceit.
31.6: A theme: 'let me be weighed in an honest balance, so that God may know my integrity' — the appeal to divine assessment. The NT's most searching passage on divine assessment is 2 Cor 5:10: 'we must all appear before the judgment seat of Christ.' But those in Christ appear wearing his righteousness, not their own — the balance is honest, and the one who stands in their place has already been weighed.
31.7: A theme: 'if my heart has followed my eyes, or any stain has clung to my hand' — the moral contamination that spreads from sight to heart to deed. The NT's diagnosis is the same: 'out of the heart come evil thoughts, murder, adultery...' (Matt 15:19). The sequence Job fears — sight corrupting heart corrupting action — is the sequence the gospel addresses at its root, by transforming the heart.
31.8: A theme: 'let me plant and another harvest' — the imprecation of displacement. The NT's positive form: 'one sows and another reaps' (John 4:37) describes the missionary pattern where labor benefits those who did not labor. The displacement Job invokes as curse becomes, in the gospel's economy, a description of the generous overflow of blessing.
31.9: A theme: the prohibition against adultery — 'if my heart has been drawn away by a woman.' Job's oath of marital fidelity is the OT foundation of the NT's teaching on covenant marriage (Matt 19:4-6, Eph 5:25-33). Christ's faithfulness to his bride, the church, is the theological grounding of the marital covenant Job here affirms.
31.10: A shadow: 'let my wife grind for another man' — the self-imprecation of maximum social shame. The NT transforms the marriage metaphor: Christ's relationship to the church is that of the faithful husband (Eph 5:25-27), who 'loved the church and gave himself up for her.' The shame Job invokes for infidelity is the mirror-image of the faithfulness Christ embodies.
31.11: A revelation of God: 'for that would be a shameful outrage and an iniquity deserving judgment' — the naming of sexual sin as a breach of justice, not merely decorum. The NT confirms this: 'flee from sexual immorality' is the command, grounded in the theology of the body as the temple of the Holy Spirit (1 Cor 6:18-20). The body belongs to God, which is why its violation is an iniquity.
31.12: A shadow: 'a fire that burns all the way to Abaddon' — the consequence of lust reaching into the realm of destruction. The NT's warning is parallel: 'if your right eye causes you to sin, tear it out and throw it away. For it is better that you lose one of your members than that your whole body be thrown into hell' (Matt 5:29). The fire that reaches Abaddon is the same fire Christ warns against.
31.13: A revelation of God: 'if I have dismissed the complaint of my servant or maidservant when they had a dispute with me' — the obligation of justice to those under one's authority. The NT grounds this in the incarnation: God himself took the form of a servant (Phil 2:7), making every servant's complaint a matter of divine concern. The master who dismisses a servant's case dismisses it before the one who became a servant.
31.14: A revelation of God: 'what could I do when God rises up? when he holds inquiry, what shall I answer him?' — the anticipation of divine scrutiny. The NT's comfort: those in Christ 'will not come into judgment' (John 5:24) — the inquiry that Job fears as exposing potential failure has already been conducted, and the verdict rendered: no condemnation (Rom 8:1).
31.15: A revelation of God: 'did not the one who made me in the womb make him too? did not one God form us both?' — the common humanity of master and servant, grounded in common creation. The NT's fullest expression: 'there is neither slave nor free... for you are all one in Christ Jesus' (Gal 3:28). The equality Job asserts creatorially, Paul grounds in the new creation inaugurated by Christ.
31.16: A revelation of God: 'if I have refused the poor what they needed, or caused the widow's eyes to fail' — the obligation to the poor and widow. The NT names care for the poor and orphan as 'religion that is pure and undefiled' (Jas 1:27). Christ's own ministry was characterized by concern for the poor (Luke 4:18); his body continues it.
31.17: A revelation of God: 'if I ate my food alone and the fatherless ate nothing' — the obligation to share one's table. The NT's eucharistic community is defined by this sharing: the early church had 'all things in common... distributing to each as any had need' (Acts 2:44-45). The closed table Job swears against is the antithesis of Christ's open one.
31.18: A revelation of God: 'from my youth the fatherless grew up with me as with a father' — a lifetime of care for the vulnerable. The NT names God as 'Father of the fatherless' (Ps 68:5) and grounds adoption into his family in Christ's work (Rom 8:15). Job's fatherly care for the fatherless mirrors the divine fatherhood that the gospel makes accessible.
31.19: A theme: 'if I have seen anyone dying from lack of clothing' — the obligation to clothe the naked. The NT's judgment scene turns on exactly this: 'I was naked and you clothed me... as you did it to one of the least of these my brothers, you did it to me' (Matt 25:36,40). The naked stranger is Christ; Job's oath is the life Christ commends at the judgment.
31.20: A revelation of God: 'if the wool of my sheep has not kept him warm' — care that comes from one's own resources. The NT's good shepherd 'lays down his life for the sheep' (John 10:11) — giving not just wool but himself. Job's generous self-giving from his own resources is the creaturely form of the divine self-giving in Christ.
31.21: A shadow: 'if I have raised my hand against the fatherless when I had allies at the city gate' — the abuse of power against the defenseless. The NT's condemnation of power used against the vulnerable is sharp (Luke 20:47: scribes who 'devour widows' houses'). Christ, who had all power, used it entirely on behalf of the defenseless.
31.22: A shadow: 'let my arm fall from its socket and be broken from the elbow' — the self-imprecation against arm-violence. The NT's parallel: Christ stretched out his arms on the cross — not to strike but to bear.
