The arms that Job swears will be broken if used unjustly were, in Christ, stretched out in the ultimate act of non-retaliatory self-giving.
31.23: A revelation of God: 'for the destruction God sends is a terror to me; before his majesty I cannot hold my ground' — the divine majesty as the ground of Job's ethical restraint. The NT's basis for ethics is the same: 'since we know the fear of the Lord, we persuade others' (2 Cor 5:11). The awe that motivates Job's integrity is taken up into the NT's theology of the divine majesty revealed in Christ.
31.24: A theme: 'if I have made gold my hope or called pure gold my security' — the prohibition of wealth as ultimate trust. The NT's parallel: 'you cannot serve God and money' (Matt 6:24); 'it is easier for a camel to go through the eye of a needle than for a rich person to enter the kingdom of God' (Matt 19:24). Job's oath against making gold his security is the OT form of Christ's teaching on mammon.
31.25: A theme: 'if I rejoiced that my wealth was great or that my hand had gained much' — the spiritual danger of satisfaction in abundance. The NT's beatitude is the reversal: 'blessed are the poor in spirit, for theirs is the kingdom of heaven' (Matt 5:3). The one who does not rejoice in his own hand's gain can receive the kingdom as gift.
31.26: A theme: 'if I gazed at the sun in its brilliance or the moon as it moved in splendor' — the prohibition of star and sun worship. The NT places Christ above all cosmic objects: he is 'the bright morning star' (Rev 22:16), and in the new creation 'the city has no need of sun or moon to shine on it, for the glory of God gives it light, and its lamp is the Lamb' (Rev 21:23). The objects of potential idolatry are subordinate to the one the gospel reveals.
31.27: A theme: 'my heart was secretly seduced, and my hand was kissed toward them' — the inner movement toward idolatry, the heart drawn away before the hand acts. The NT locates idolatry in the heart: 'covetousness... is idolatry' (Col 3:5). The secret seduction of the heart toward anything other than God is the root of idolatry that the gospel addresses by redirecting the heart's affection toward Christ.
31.28: A revelation of God: 'for I would have denied the God above' — the equation of cosmic idolatry with the denial of the true God. The NT's confession is the positive form: 'Jesus is Lord' (Rom 10:9) — the confession that the world's objects of idolatry (wealth, power, cosmic forces) do not hold final allegiance. To confess Christ is to deny the idols.
31.29: A revelation of God: 'if I rejoiced when my enemy was ruined or exulted when disaster found him' — the prohibition of schadenfreude. The NT's command is the positive version: 'love your enemies and pray for those who persecute you' (Matt 5:44). Christ prays from the cross for those who are executing him (Luke 23:34). Job's oath against enemy-joy is the minimum; Christ's active love for enemies is the fulfillment.
31.30: A revelation of God: 'I have not let my mouth sin by calling down a curse on his life' — the restrained tongue that does not curse enemies. The NT's positive: 'bless those who curse you, pray for those who abuse you' (Luke 6:28). Christ himself is the example: 'when he was reviled, he did not revile in return; when he suffered, he did not threaten' (1 Pet 2:23). Job's non-cursing is the threshold; Christ's active blessing is the fulfillment.
31.31: A revelation of God: 'who has not been filled with his food?' — the testimony of household generosity, confirmed by Job's own servants. The feeding motif is central to Christ's ministry: the miraculous feedings (Mark 6:42: 'they all ate and were satisfied'), the parables of banquets, the eucharist. The question 'who has not been filled?' is answered by those who ate with Jesus.
31.32: A revelation of God: 'no stranger had to sleep in the street; I opened my doors to the traveler' — radical hospitality. The NT's extension: 'do not neglect to show hospitality to strangers, for thereby some have entertained angels unawares' (Heb 13:2). Christ himself was 'a stranger' — 'he came to his own, and his own people did not receive him' (John 1:11) — but those who receive him receive the Father (Matt 10:40).
31.33: A type: 'if I have hidden my transgressions like Adam, concealing my iniquity in my heart' — the Adamic pattern of concealment after sin. Genesis 3:8-10 is the prototype: Adam hides from God after the fall. Christ is the second Adam (Rom 5:14-15) who does not hide but fully discloses himself: 'I am the way and the truth and the life' (John 14:6). Concealment is the Adamic response; transparent self-giving is the Christic reversal.
31.34: A revelation of God: 'because I feared the crowd and the contempt of families terrified me, so I kept silent' — the capitulation to social pressure that Job swears he has avoided. The NT's opposite is the martyr-witness: 'do not fear those who kill the body' (Matt 10:28). Christ before Pilate does not keep silent to preserve himself; he speaks truth to power (John 18:36-37). The fear Job overcame in his integrity, Christ overcame in his Passion.
31.35: A type: 'O that someone would hear me! here is my signature — let the Almighty answer me!' — the legal demand for a divine response. The divine speeches in chapters 38-41 answer this demand, though not in the way Job expected. In the NT, the ultimate divine answer to humanity's legal demand is the cross: God speaks in the death and resurrection of his Son. The signature Job produces is answered by the one who bears scars in hands and side (John 20:27).
31.36: A shadow: 'I would carry it on my shoulder and wear it as a crown on my head' — the paradox of bearing one's indictment as a mark of honor. The NT's parallel: the crown of thorns (John 19:2) placed mockingly on Christ's head becomes, in the resurrection's light, the sign of his true kingship. What is given in contempt is transformed into honor.
31.37: A shadow: 'I would approach him like a prince' — the confidence of approaching God as one entitled to his audience. The NT's language for the believer's access to God: 'with confidence draw near to the throne of grace' (Heb 4:16), not as a suppliant begging for attention but as those who have a right of access through Christ. Job's princely confidence is available to those who come through the one who is himself the Prince of Peace (Isa 9:6).
31.38: A theme: 'if my land has cried out against me and its furrows have wept' — the land bearing witness to its owner's conduct. The NT holds that creation itself testifies (Rom 1:20) and groans (Rom 8:22); the land's witness is real. The creation that cries out against those who abuse it waits for 'the revealing of the sons of God' (Rom 8:19) — the restoration Christ will bring.
31.39: A revelation of God: 'if I have eaten its produce without paying, or stripped the life from its owners' — the economic obligation of just dealing. The NT's equivalent: Zacchaeus, transformed by Jesus, restores fourfold what he has taken (Luke 19:8). The justice of honest economic dealing is what the encounter with Christ produces, not merely commands.
31.40: A theme: 'then let thistles grow instead of wheat, and weeds instead of barley' — the curse of the ground as the consequence of injustice. Genesis 3:17-18 is the background: thorns and thistles are the curse-sign of the fall. The NT holds that Christ 'redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us' (Gal 3:13) — he wore the thorns as his crown. The words of Job are ended at this verse, in a fitting image of what Christ absorbed.

32.1: A theme: 'so these three men stopped answering Job, because he was righteous in his own eyes' — the paralysis of conventional wisdom before an anomalous case. The three friends could not answer because their theology lacked the categories for innocent suffering. The NT supplies the missing category: the suffering of the righteous is redemptive, not merely disciplinary, when joined to Christ's.
32.2: A theme: Elihu's anger 'burned against Job, because he declared himself righteous rather than God' — the accusation that Job's insistence on his own righteousness displaces God's. The NT navigates this tension: the doctrine of justification holds that the sinner is declared righteous not by his own insistence but by God's declaration through Christ. Job's self-defense, vindicated in 42:7, is not self-righteousness — it is honest assessment of his actual integrity.
32.3: A revelation of God: Elihu 'was also angry with the three friends because they had found no answer and yet had condemned Job' — he perceives the injustice of condemnation without proof. The NT's standard: 'judge not by appearances, but judge with right judgment' (John 7:24). The friends condemned without evidence; Elihu at least recognizes that evidence has not been produced.
32.4: A theme: Elihu 'had waited to speak... because the others were older than he' — deference to age as a wisdom convention. The NT both honors and qualifies this: 'do not let anyone despise you for your youth' (1 Tim 4:12); 'out of the mouth of infants and nursing babies you have prepared praise' (Matt 21:16). Wisdom is not the exclusive possession of the aged.
32.5: A theme: 'when Elihu saw that the three men had no answer, his anger was aroused' — the recognition that the conventional wisdom tradition has exhausted itself. The NT describes a similar moment: the religious leaders, questioned by Jesus about the baptism of John, 'knew not what to answer' (Mark 11:33). When conventional frameworks fail to answer, a new word is needed.
32.6: A theme: 'I am young and you are old; that is why I was afraid and held back' — the fear of speaking out of proper order. The NT's prophetic pattern overrides social hierarchy when the Spirit impels: 'when they arrest you and deliver you over, do not be anxious beforehand what you are to say, but say whatever is given you in that hour' (Mark 13:11). The Spirit speaks through those who defer, when deference reaches its limit.
32.7: A theme: 'I thought, let the aged speak; let many years teach wisdom' — the assumption that age accumulates wisdom. The NT confirms the honor due to elders (1 Tim 5:17) while also holding that wisdom is a gift of the Spirit, not a product of years (1 Cor 12:8). Both are true; neither is absolute.
32.8: A revelation of God: 'but it is the Spirit in a person — the breath of the Almighty — that gives them understanding' — the Spirit as the giver of wisdom, independent of human credentials. The NT's Pentecost theology is the full expression of this: 'I will pour out my Spirit on all flesh, and your sons and daughters shall prophesy' (Acts 2:17). The understanding Elihu describes as the Spirit's gift to individuals becomes, at Pentecost, the Spirit's gift to the whole community.
32.9: A revelation of God: 'it is not the great who are always wise, nor the aged who always understand' — the democratization of wisdom through the Spirit. The NT's corroboration: 'God chose what is foolish in the world to shame the wise; God chose what is weak in the world to shame the strong' (1 Cor 1:27). The Spirit bypasses credentials and status.
32.10: A theme: 'therefore I say: listen to me; I too will express what I think' — the claim to a hearing on the basis of Spirit-given insight rather than social status. The NT's pattern of prophetic speech is similar: 'we cannot but speak of what we have seen and heard' (Acts 4:20). The compulsion to speak comes from the reality of what has been received, not from earned authority.
32.11: A theme: 'I listened carefully to your arguments while you searched for what to say' — the patient attending to all that has been said before adding one's own voice. The NT's wisdom: 'let every person be quick to hear, slow to speak, slow to anger' (Jas 1:19). Elihu embodies the listening before speaking that wisdom requires.
32.12: A revelation of God: 'not one of you has refuted Job or answered what he said' — the honest assessment that conventional theology has not provided a real answer. The NT identifies the real answer: the cross and resurrection supply what no theodicy argument can — not an explanation of suffering but the presence of God in suffering and the power of God through it.
32.13: A theme: 'do not say, we have found wisdom; let God deal with him' — the warning against deflecting to God as a way of avoiding the hard intellectual work. The NT's parallel: the disciples who asked 'who sinned, this man or his parents?' (John 9:2) were similarly attempting to route the uncomfortable case into a pre-formed theological category. Jesus rejected the deflection and addressed the situation directly.
32.14: A theme: 'he has not directed his argument against me, and I will not reply to him with your kind of arguments' — Elihu's commitment to engage afresh rather than repeat the failed approach. The NT's pattern: Jesus taught 'as one who had authority, and not as their scribes' (Matt 7:29) — not reprising the existing framework but speaking from a different ground entirely.
32.15: A revelation of God: 'they are astonished; they say nothing more; words have left them' — the exhaustion of human wisdom before the problem of innocent suffering. The NT's response is not a better argument but a person: 'in these last days he has spoken to us by his Son' (Heb 1:2). When words have left human wisdom, God speaks his final word in flesh.
32.16: A theme: 'I waited, but they did not speak; they stood there and answered no more' — the silence that precedes the new voice. In the NT, silence precedes the new word: the silence of Zechariah before the angel (Luke 1:20), the silence of Jesus before Pilate (Matt 27:14), and ultimately the silence of the tomb before Easter morning. Silence is not the end; it is the space before the decisive speech.
32.17: A theme: 'I too will give my answer; I too will state what I think' — the moment of prophetic declaration. The NT holds that Spirit-impelled speech is not merely opinion-sharing: 'the Holy Spirit will teach you in that very hour what you ought to say' (Luke 12:12). Elihu's 'what I think' is not mere opinion; he believes he speaks by the Spirit (32:8).
32.18: A revelation of God: 'I am full of words; the Spirit within me urges me on' — the prophetic compulsion to speak. The NT's parallel: Jeremiah's 'there is in my heart as it were a burning fire shut up in my bones' (Jer 20:9); the disciples who 'cannot but speak' (Acts 4:20). The Spirit's fullness presses outward; the contained word demands release.
32.19: A shadow: 'my belly is like wine with no outlet — like new wineskins about to burst' — the pressure of uncontained speech. The NT's new wine imagery: 'no one puts new wine into old wineskins. If he does, the wine will burst the skins' (Mark 2:22). The new teaching that Jesus brings has exactly this character — it cannot be contained in the old forms. Elihu's image anticipates the NT's description of the gospel's pressure on old structures.
32.20: A theme: 'I will speak and find relief; I will open my lips and answer' — the therapeutic necessity of faithful speech. The NT's parallel is testimony: 'for with the heart one believes and is justified, and with the mouth one confesses and is saved' (Rom 10:10). Speaking what one has received is not just transmission but a form of receiving it more fully.
32.21: A revelation of God: 'I will show partiality to no one, nor will I flatter anyone' — the impartiality that the Spirit-given voice must maintain. The NT grounds divine impartiality in God's own character: 'God shows no partiality' (Rom 2:11; Acts 10:34). The judge of all is impartial; the witness who speaks for him must be as well.
32.22: A revelation of God: 'for I do not know how to flatter; if I did, my Maker would soon remove me' — the accountability of the prophetic witness before his Maker. The NT's equivalent: 'we must all appear before the judgment seat of Christ, so that each one may receive what is due for what he has done in the body' (2 Cor 5:10). The one who flatters rather than speaks truth has disqualified himself before his Maker.

33.1: Elihu speaks directly to Job — a shift from the three friends who addressed each other and the audience. Christ also speaks directly and personally to the individual (John 11:23: “Your brother will rise again”; John 20:16: “Mary”). The personal address is the mark of genuine mediation.
33.2: Elihu has opened his mouth; his tongue speaks within his mouth. The self-announcement of the speaker prepares for the greater announcement: the Word made flesh (John 1:14), the one whose speech comes not from a human mouth alone but from the Father who sent him (John 12:49).
33.3: Words from an upright heart; lips speak what he knows sincerely. Elihu’s claim of integrity anticipates Christ’s perfect sincerity: “the words that I say to you I do not speak on my own authority, but the Father who dwells in me does his works” (John 14:10). Christ’s words are perfectly aligned with his character.
33.4: “The Spirit of God has made me, and the breath of the Almighty gives me life.” Elihu acknowledges his creaturely dependence on the divine breath — the same nšmāh of Gen 2:7. Christ’s resurrection is the new creation: he breathes on his disciples (John 20:22), giving the Holy Spirit — the life-giving breath now becomes the Spirit of new creation.
33.5: If you can answer me, set your words in order before me and take your stand. The call to make a case — and then present Christ as the one whose case stands without defect: 1 Pet 3:15 “always be prepared to make a defense to anyone who asks you for a reason for the hope that is in you.” Christ is both the advocate and the one whose case cannot be refuted (John 8:46: “which one of you convicts me of sin?”).
33.6: “See, I stand before God on your behalf as you wished; I too was formed from clay.” Elihu’s claim to be a fellow-creature who stands before God on Job’s behalf is the imperfect anticipation of Christ, who is also “made from dust” in the Incarnation (Phil 2:7: “born in the likeness of men”) and stands before the Father as humanity’s representative (Heb 2:17: “he had to be made like his brothers in every respect... to make propitiation for the sins of the people”).
33.7: My dread will not terrify you; my pressure will not be heavy upon you. The gentle approach of the true mediator — Christ who says “my yoke is easy and my burden is light” (Matt 11:30) and “do not be afraid” (John 6:20). Unlike the approach to Sinai (Heb 12:18–21), the approach through Christ brings confidence not terror.
33.8: Elihu reports what Job said — the careful listening that precedes a genuine answer. Christ also hears before he speaks: Heb 5:7 “in the days of his flesh, Jesus offered up prayers and supplications with loud cries and tears to him who was able to save him from death, and he was heard because of his reverence.”
33.9: “You said: I am clean, without transgression; I am innocent and there is no iniquity in me.” Job’s claim of innocence, which is contested by the friends, is ultimately vindicated by God (42:7). But Christ’s claim of sinlessness is not contested — it is absolute: “In him there is no sin” (1 John 3:5); “one who in every respect has been tempted as we are, yet without sin” (Heb 4:15).
33.10: But God finds grievances against me; regards me as his enemy. The estrangement Job experiences — God as adversary — is what Christ absorbs at the cross. He becomes the enemy of God on behalf of those who were God’s enemies (Rom 5:10: “while we were enemies we were reconciled to God by the death of his Son”).
33.11: He puts my feet in stocks; he watches all my paths. The prisoner-of-God image. Christ is himself the prisoner at his arrest (John 18:12) — the free one bound so that the bound might go free. His paths are watched by the Father because they lead infallibly to redemption.
33.12: “In this I tell you plainly that you are wrong: God is greater than any human being.” The divine greatness that exceeds human comprehension is the point where Elihu’s theology is correct — and it is the premise of the Incarnation: only one who is greater than any human being could be the mediator that both Job and the cosmos need (1 Tim 2:5: “one mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus”).
33.13: Why do you argue against him? He does not give account for any of his actions. The inscrutable freedom of God that exceeds all human questioning — and yet: the cross is God’s self-account, his explanation, his justification of his own ways (Rom 3:25–26: “it was to show his righteousness at the present time, so that he might be just and the justifier of the one who has faith in Jesus”).
33.14: God speaks in one way and then another, though people do not notice. The various modes of divine revelation enumerated here (dreams, visions, chastening) all anticipate the final speech: Heb 1:1–2 “Long ago, at many times and in many ways, God spoke to our fathers by the prophets, but in these last days he has spoken to us by his Son.”
33.15: In a dream, in a nighttime vision, when deep sleep falls on people. God speaks through the unconscious, reaching the person when all defenses are down. The incarnation is the most direct form of this speaking: God entering the full human condition — including the vulnerability of sleep (Matt 8:24: Jesus sleeps in the boat; Luke 2:7: the infant sleeps).
33.16: He opens people’s ears and confirms what he is teaching them. The opened ear of the disciple: Isa 50:4–5 “morning by morning he wakens my ear to hear as those who are taught” — the Servant of the LORD whose ear is opened is the pattern Christ embodies; and he opens the disciples’ understanding (Luke 24:45).
33.17: To turn them from their intended course and shield them from pride. The purpose of divine discipline: turning and humbling. Christ’s teaching also turns people from the way of destruction (Matt 7:13–14) and his cross is the supreme destruction of human pride (1 Cor 1:29: “so that no human being might boast in the presence of God”).
33.18: He keeps him from the pit and his life from crossing into death. God’s preserving purpose in suffering. The ultimate keeping-from-the-pit is the resurrection of Christ: Acts 2:24 “God raised him up, loosing the pangs of death, because it was not possible for him to be held by it” (citing Ps 16:10: “you will not abandon my soul to Sheol”).
33.19: Chastened with pain upon his bed; unending conflict in his bones. Elihu describes the physical suffering of God’s discipline. Heb 5:8: “although he was a son, he learned obedience through what he suffered.” Christ himself is the suffering-and-learning servant, though his suffering is not punitive but redemptive.
33.20: He cannot eat bread; appetite revolts against any food. The fast of suffering. Jesus himself fasted forty days (Matt 4:2) and on the cross refused the drugged wine (Mark 15:23). He abstained from earthly sustenance to accomplish the work of redemption, offering himself as the true bread (John 6:51).
33.21: His flesh wastes away until it is gone; bones that were hidden now protrude. The bodily dissolution of extreme suffering. The crucified Christ: John 19:34 (blood and water from his side); Ps 22:14–17 (bones out of joint, strength dried up, feet and hands pierced) — the Passion Psalm describes this exact wasting.
33.22: His life draws near the pit and his soul to the agents of death. The proximity to death — and then the mediator arrives. Christ descended to the deepest point — Eph 4:9 “he had also descended into the lower parts of the earth” — so that his rescue could reach those nearest the pit.
33.23: “But if there is a messenger for him, a mediator (meliṣ) — one in a thousand — who declares to the person their uprightness.” This is the book’s most explicit mediator-term outside 19:25. The meliṣ (interpreter, messenger, mediator) who is one-in-a-thousand finds its fulfilment in Christ: “there is one mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus” (1 Tim 2:5). He alone, of all possible advocates, both declares the person’s uprightness and has grounds to do so.
33.24: “Then God is gracious to him and says: Spare him from going down to the pit; I have found a ransom (kōper).” The ransom-declaration from God is the theological heart of the chapter. Mark 10:45: “the Son of Man came not to be served but to serve, and to give his life as a ransom (lytron) for many.” Heb 9:15: Christ “is the mediator of a new covenant, so that those who are called may receive the promised eternal inheritance, since a death has occurred that redeems them from the transgressions committed under the first covenant.” The ransom God finds is his own Son.
33.25: “His flesh becomes fresher than a child’s; he is restored to the days of his youth.” The restoration through the mediator’s ransom — a new-creation freshness beyond the original state. Tit 3:5: “he saved us through the washing of regeneration and renewing of the Holy Spirit.” John 3:3: “unless one is born again he cannot see the kingdom of God.” The born-again metaphor is the new-covenant form of the flesh-fresher-than-a-child image Elihu describes.
33.26: “He prays to God and God accepts him; he sees his face with a shout of joy, and God restores his righteousness to him.” The restoration pattern: prayer, acceptance, sight-of-God, righteousness restored. 1 John 3:2: “when he appears we shall be like him, because we shall see him as he is.” The face-to-face vision is the eschatological goal, secured through the mediator’s ransom.
33.27: He looks on people and says: “I sinned and bent what was right, and it brought me nothing.” The confession that follows rescue rather than preceding it — the person acknowledges sin in the light of grace. 1 John 1:9: “if we confess our sins, he is faithful and just to forgive us our sins and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness.” The confession that brings forgiveness is possible only because the ransom has been paid.
33.28: “God will deliver his life from the pit, and his life will see the light.” The seeing-of-light after rescue from the pit is the resurrection pattern. John 1:4–5: “in him was life, and the life was the light of men. The light shines in the darkness, and the darkness has not overcome it.” The pit cannot hold the one the mediator has ransomed.
33.29: God does all these things — again and again — with a person. The repeated, patient, redeeming work of God. 2 Pet 3:9: “the Lord is not slow to fulfill his promise as some count slowness, but is patient toward you, not wishing that any should perish, but that all should reach repentance.” The cross is the once-for-all event that grounds the again-and-again patience.
33.30: To bring his life back from the pit, that he might be lit by the light of the living. The purpose of redemption: not merely rescue from death but lighting-up by life. Christ as “the resurrection and the life” (John 11:25) brings those rescued from the pit into a new quality of existence — the light of the living that is nothing less than Christ himself (John 8:12).
33.31: Pay close attention, Job; be silent and I will speak. The invitation to listening silence before the mediator’s word. Heb 1:2 God “has spoken to us by his Son” — the final word that requires the silence of all other speech, the attentive listening of faith.
33.32: If you have anything to say, answer me; speak up, for I want to see you vindicated. Elihu genuinely desires Job’s vindication — not his condemnation. Christ, the perfect Mediator, desires the vindication of all who come to him: “whoever comes to me I will never cast out” (John 6:37); “I did not come to judge the world but to save the world” (John 12:47).
33.33: If not, listen to me; be silent, and I will teach you wisdom. The teaching of wisdom to the silent one. Matt 11:29: “take my yoke upon you and learn from me, for I am gentle and lowly in heart, and you will find rest for your souls.” The wisdom Christ teaches is himself — the one the wisdom poem of ch. 28 was searching for.

34.1: Elihu shifts audience — from Job to the wise men in the assembly. Christ also modulates his teaching audience (crowds, disciples, Pharisees, individuals) without modifying his message. The gospel is for all who have ears to hear (Matt 13:9).
34.2: Hear my words, O wise men; listen to me, you who have knowledge. 1 Cor 1:26–27: “not many of you were wise according to worldly standards... God chose what is foolish in the world to shame the wise.” The audience Elihu addresses — the wise and knowledgeable — are precisely those who find the cross a stumbling block (1 Cor 1:23).
34.3: For the ear tests words as the palate tastes food. The discernment of speech: 1 Cor 2:15 “the spiritual person judges all things, but is himself to be judged by no one.” Christ’s words are the standard by which all other words are tested: John 12:48 “the word that I have spoken will judge him on the last day.”
34.4: Let us choose together what is right. The communal discernment process that Elihu proposes — but true discernment requires the Spirit: 1 Cor 2:10–11 “the Spirit searches everything, even the depths of God.” The right that is truly right is revealed in Christ who is himself righteousness (1 Cor 1:30).
34.5: For Job has said, “I am righteous, and God has denied me my right.” Elihu summarizes Job’s complaint accurately. God’s ultimate answer is not to deny Job’s claim but to vindicate it (42:7) — and the cross is the place where God proves he does not deny the right of the innocent: he vindicates them through resurrection.
34.6: Must I lie about my case? My wound is past healing though I have done no wrong. The wound of the innocent sufferer that surpasses human healing — this is the condition Christ enters. Isa 53:5: “by his wounds we are healed” — the wound that is past human healing becomes the source of healing for all.
34.7: What man is like Job, who drinks mockery like water? The exceptional sufferer who endures mockery as if it were daily drink. Ps 69:21: “they gave me poison for food, and for my thirst they gave me sour wine to drink” — applied in John 19:29 to Christ who literally drank the sour wine of mockery on the cross.
34.8: Who keeps company with evildoers and walks with wicked men. The accusation against Job — which is also the accusation against Jesus: “this man receives sinners and eats with them” (Luke 15:2). The one who walks with the wicked to redeem them is not contaminated but transforms them.
34.9: For Job said, “A man gains nothing by delighting in God.” Elihu quotes Job at his worst moment. But Christ’s resurrection is God’s refutation of this despair: “your labor in the Lord is not in vain” (1 Cor 15:58); the delight in God that Job doubts in extremis is vindicated by Easter.
34.10: “Far be it from God to do evil, far from the Almighty to do wrong.” The moral purity of God that undergirds the cross: God was not unjust in punishing Christ; he was satisfying justice on behalf of sinners who could not satisfy it themselves. Rom 3:25–26: the cross demonstrates that God is “just and the justifier of the one who has faith in Jesus.”
34.11: He repays each person according to their work and deals with everyone according to their conduct. The justice principle: 2 Cor 5:10; Rev 20:12. But the gospel exceeds mere retribution — Rom 4:5–6 credits Christ’s righteousness to the one who does not work but trusts. The one who repays according to works takes the penalty himself in Christ.
34.12: Of a truth, God will not act wickedly; the Almighty will not pervert justice. The cross is the proof: God did not pervert justice by letting sin go unpunished. Rom 3:26: “at the present time his righteousness might be demonstrated, so that he might be just and the justifier.” The cross satisfies justice; it does not bypass it.
34.13: Who gave God his charge over the earth? Who appointed him over the whole world? The rhetorical questions assert God’s aseity and sovereign lordship. Col 1:16: “by him all things were created... all things were created through him and for him.” God owns creation because he made it; and Christ is both the Creator and the appointed heir (Heb 1:2).
34.14: “If he should set his heart against us and gather to himself his Spirit and breath.” The contingency of all creaturely life on continued divine sustenance. Heb 1:3: Christ “upholds the universe by the word of his power.” Col 1:17: “in him all things hold together.” The withdrawal Elihu imagines is the condition that only Christ’s sustaining prevents.
34.15: All flesh would perish together; human beings would return to dust. The fragility of existence without continuous divine support. Christ as the one in whom all things consist (Col 1:17) is the answer to this fragility — and his own resurrection from the dust is the pledge that human bodies will not be abandoned to it permanently (1 Cor 15:42–44).
34.16: If you have understanding, hear this; listen to what I am saying. The summons to understanding — which comes finally not from Elihu but from the divine speech and ultimately from Christ who gives understanding: Luke 24:45 “then he opened their minds to understand the Scriptures.”
34.17: “Shall one who hates justice govern? Will you condemn the One who is righteous and mighty?” The rhetorical challenge anticipates the accusations against Christ: Pilate’s verdict (“I find no fault in him”) and the crowd’s condemnation represent the condemning of the one who is righteous. But Isa 9:7: “of the increase of his government and peace there will be no end” — the righteous and mighty one does govern.
34.18: Is it right to say to a king, “You are worthless,” and to nobles, “You are wicked”? How much less is it right to accuse God? At the crucifixion the soldiers mocked: “Hail, King of the Jews” (John 19:3) with contempt. But Phil 2:9–11 declares that this king will be acknowledged without ambiguity by every tongue.
34.19: He shows no partiality to princes and does not favor the rich over the poor, for they are all his handiwork. Acts 10:34: “God shows no partiality.” The cross is for all: John 3:16 “God so loved the world” — not one class or nation. The gospel reaches both the thief on the cross and the Roman centurion who guards him.
34.20: In a moment they die; at midnight the people are convulsed and pass away; the mighty are removed without hand. The sudden reversals of death — Christ promises the same reversal in reverse: at his return the dead in Christ will be raised “in a moment, in the twinkling of an eye” (1 Cor 15:52).
34.21: His eyes are on the ways of everyone; he sees all their steps. Heb 4:13: “no creature is hidden from his sight, but all are naked and exposed to the eyes of him to whom we must give account.” This omniscience makes both judgment inescapable and rescue certain — God sees the suffering of the afflicted (v28) and the wickedness of oppressors with equal clarity.
34.22: There is no darkness or deep shadow where evildoers can hide themselves. The absolute transparency before God. John 3:20: “everyone who does wicked things hates the light and does not come to the light, lest his works should be exposed.” Christ is the light that unmasks — and also the grace that forgives when the exposed person comes (1 John 1:7).
34.23: He does not impose on anyone more than is warranted to bring them before God in judgment. God’s measured justice — the cross is the most precise expression of this: “the wages of sin is death” (Rom 6:23), and Christ bore exactly what was warranted, no more and no less. God’s justice is not arbitrary but precisely proportional.
34.24: He shatters the mighty without investigation and sets others in their place. The divine reversals of power that the incarnation executes: Luke 1:52 “he has brought down the mighty from their thrones and exalted those of humble estate” — the Magnificat’s announcement of the reversal that Christ’s coming enacts.
34.25: He knows their deeds and overturns them in the night; they are crushed. The night of crucifixion is both the darkest night and the moment of the decisive overturn: John 13:27 “what you are going to do, do quickly” — Judas goes out and “it was night” (13:30). But the one crushed rises; the night of his death becomes the dawn of resurrection.
34.26: He strikes them for their wickedness in an open place where others can see. The public nature of divine judgment. Christ’s crucifixion was public — outside the city gate (Heb 13:12), on a hill visible to all — where the bearing of the world’s sin was enacted not in secret but in full view.
34.27: Because they turned away from following him and gave no thought to any of his ways. The root of judgment is departure from God. The cross is God’s call-back: “Come to me, all who labor and are heavy laden” (Matt 11:28); “I, when I am lifted up from the earth, will draw all people to myself” (John 12:32). The one from whom people turn away now draws them to himself.
34.28: “So that they brought the cry of the poor before him, and he heard the outcry of the afflicted.” God hears the cry of the poor. Luke 4:18: “he has anointed me to proclaim good news to the poor.” Matt 25:40: “as you did it to one of the least of these my brothers, you did it to me.” The divine hearing of the afflicted culminates in Christ who is himself afflicted on their behalf.
34.29: “When he is quiet, who can condemn? When he hides his face, who can see him?” The question that anticipates Paul’s answer: Rom 8:33–34 “who shall bring any charge against God’s elect? It is God who justifies. Who is to condemn? Christ Jesus is the one who died — more than that, who was raised, who is at the right hand of God, who indeed is interceding for us.” When God is at peace with us through Christ, no condemnation can stand.
34.30: Lest a godless person reign and ensnare the people. The threat of ungodly rule. Christ is the ruler who does not ensnare but liberates: John 8:36 “if the Son sets you free, you will be free indeed.” Rev 20:10: the ultimate ensnarer — the devil — is thrown into the lake of fire; Christ’s reign excludes the godless one.
34.31: Has anyone said to God, “I have endured my punishment; I will not sin any more”? The posture of repentance before God. Luke 15:18–19: “I will arise and go to my father, and I will say to him, Father, I have sinned against heaven and before you.” Christ’s parable depicts the Father running to receive this confession — grace meets the returning penitent.
34.32: “Teach me what I cannot see; if I have done wrong, I will not do it again.” The teachable heart that asks God to reveal its blind spots. John 16:8: the Spirit “will convict the world concerning sin and righteousness and judgment” — Christ sends the Spirit who teaches what we cannot see and enables the repentance Elihu envisions.
34.33: Should God repay on your terms because you reject his? For you must choose. The ultimacy of the choice: Josh 24:15 “choose this day whom you will serve”; Rev 22:17 “let the one who is thirsty come; let the one who desires take the water of life without price.” Christ does not force; he offers — and the choice is ours.
34.34: People of understanding will agree with me; every wise person who hears me will say. The consensus of the wise — but 1 Cor 1:20–21: “where is the wise person?... God made foolish the wisdom of the world. For since, in the wisdom of God, the world did not know God through wisdom, it pleased God through the folly of what we preach to save those who believe.”
34.35: “Job speaks without knowledge; his words are without insight.” Elihu’s condemnation is too harsh — God himself overturns this verdict in ch. 42. Christ’s verdicts are the ones that stand: “blessed are the pure in heart” (Matt 5:8); “well done, good and faithful servant” (Matt 25:21). The final judgment of a life belongs to God alone.
34.36: Would that Job were tested to the limit, because his answers are like those of wicked men. Elihu’s excessive accusation. Christ is the one who is tested to the limit (Matt 4:1–11; Heb 4:15) and comes through without sin — demonstrating that the test can be endured in the power God provides.
34.37: For he adds rebellion to his sin; he mocks us and heaps up words against God. The final overreach of Elihu’s accusation. But God’s verdict in 42:7 shows that Job “spoke what is right.” Christ is the perfect judge of the heart (John 2:25) whose verdicts are not overturned.

35.1: Elihu continues. The speech now turns to the question of whether human behavior affects God — a question the Incarnation answers definitively: God is affected by human sin, not in the sense of being diminished, but in the sense that he chose to bear it (2 Cor 5:21).
35.2: “Do you think this is just when you say, ‘My righteousness is more than God’s’?” Elihu’s framing of Job’s complaint. The ultimate answer to the comparison between divine and human righteousness is Christ, who is both — divine righteousness incarnate (Rom 1:17) and the source of human righteousness by faith (Rom 3:22).
35.3: You say, “What does it benefit you? What do I gain from not sinning?” The cynical question that emptiness can produce. The resurrection answers it: “your labor in the Lord is not in vain” (1 Cor 15:58). The gain of righteousness is Christ himself — the surpassing worth of knowing him (Phil 3:8).
35.4: I will give you an answer, and your companions along with you. Elihu speaks to both Job and the friends. Christ speaks to the individual and to the community simultaneously: Heb 4:12 “the word of God is living and active, sharper than any two-edged sword,” piercing to the thoughts and intentions of every individual heart.
35.5: Look up at the sky and see; behold the clouds, higher than you. The call to lift the gaze heavenward. Heb 12:2: “looking to Jesus, the founder and perfecter of our faith.” The upward look is to the ascended Christ who has gone higher than the clouds — “exalted above the heavens” (Heb 7:26).
35.6: If you sin, what do you accomplish against him? Elihu’s point about divine transcendence. But the Incarnation precisely reverses this: in Christ, God IS affected by human sin — Isa 53:4 “he has borne our griefs and carried our sorrows.” The transcendent God becomes the one who absorbs sin’s consequences.
35.7: If you are righteous, what do you give him? What does he receive from your hand? The question of what humans can offer God — which is nothing self-generated. 2 Cor 5:21: “for our sake he made him to be sin who knew no sin, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God.” Christ gives the Father what humans cannot: perfect righteousness, on behalf of those who have none.
35.8: Your wickedness harms only someone like yourself; your righteousness benefits only other people. The horizontal consequences of sin and righteousness. But Christ’s righteousness has a vertical dimension: it accomplishes what no human righteousness could — the reconciliation of Creator and creature (Col 1:20).
35.9: Under the weight of great oppression people cry out; they call for help from the arm of the powerful. The universal human cry under oppression. Luke 4:18–19: “he has sent me to proclaim release to the captives... to set at liberty those who are oppressed, to proclaim the year of the Lord’s favor.” Christ is the arm of the powerful who responds to this cry.
35.10: “But no one asks, ‘Where is God my Maker, who gives songs in the night?’” The deeper question that the oppressed fail to ask. Acts 16:25: Paul and Silas in prison at midnight, singing hymns to God — the literal enactment of songs-in-the-night. Christ gives the capacity to sing in prison that Elihu identifies as the mark of true knowledge of God.
35.11: Who teaches us more than the animals of the earth and makes us wiser than the birds of the sky. The divine teaching that elevates humanity beyond the rest of creation. Matt 11:29: “take my yoke upon you and learn from me.” Col 2:3: “in him are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge.” Christ is the teacher who makes the disciple wiser than all natural wisdom.
35.12: There they cry, but he does not answer, because of the pride of evil people. The unanswered cry of those who cry from wrong motives — pride rather than genuine need. Jas 4:6: “God opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble.” Christ’s answer is to those who humble themselves — “everyone who exalts himself will be humbled, and everyone who humbles himself will be exalted” (Luke 14:11).
35.13: Certainly God does not hear a hollow cry; the Almighty does not regard it. The authentic cry that God does hear: Heb 5:7 “in the days of his flesh, Jesus offered up prayers and supplications with loud cries and tears to him who was able to save him from death, and he was heard because of his reverence.” Christ’s cry is the most genuine cry ever made — and Ps 22:24 confirms God heard it: “he has not despised or scorned the suffering of the afflicted one.”
35.14: How much less when you say you cannot see him, that the case is before him, and you must wait for him. The counsel to wait in trust. Heb 6:15: “Abraham, having patiently waited, obtained the promise.” The waiting and trusting that Elihu recommends is fulfilled in Christ who “endured the cross, despising the shame, and is seated at the right hand of the throne of God” (Heb 12:2).
35.15: But now, because God has not punished in his anger and does not take sharp notice of every transgression — The patience of God before judgment: 2 Pet 3:9: “the Lord is not slow to fulfill his promise as some count slowness, but is patient toward you, not wishing that any should perish, but that all should reach repentance.” The delay of punishment is grace extended; it finds its ultimate expression in the cross where sin is dealt with once for all.
35.16: “So Job opens his mouth with empty words; he heaps up words without knowledge.” The final judgment on Job by Elihu — which God himself overturns in ch. 42 (“Job has spoken what is right”). Christ is the one whose words are never empty: John 6:63 “the words that I have spoken to you are spirit and life.” He is the Word without emptiness, knowledge without limit.

39.1: YHWH’s opening question about the mountain goats and hinds giving birth establishes the fundamental christological frame of the divine speeches: the Creator has intimate knowledge of and care for every creature he made. Col 1:16: “by him all things were created, in heaven and on earth, visible and invisible.” The God who attends the wild birth of mountain goats in remote ranges is the God who became one of his creatures at Bethlehem — the Incarnation is the ultimate expression of Creator-attentiveness to creaturely life.
39.2: Counting the months of wild pregnancy — the divine arithmetic of creaturely gestation. Matt 10:29: “are not two sparrows sold for a penny? And not one of them will fall to the ground apart from your Father.” The sparrow-accounting is the NT echo of the mountain-goat-accounting; Christ reveals that divine attention to creaturely life is not a peripheral attribute of God but belongs to the Father’s character that the Son makes known (John 1:18: “the only God, who is at the Father’s side, he has made him known”).
39.3: The wild creatures bringing forth their young and delivering their offspring — the drama of wild birth without human attendance. John 16:21: “when a woman is giving birth, she has sorrow because her hour has come, but when she has delivered the baby, she no longer remembers the anguish, for joy that a human being has been born into the world.” Christ uses the birth metaphor to describe the eschatological labor of the church; the wild creature’s unattended birth is also the type of Christ’s own birth in the manger, without the attendance of palace or physician.
39.4: Their young becoming strong and going out, leaving and not returning — the natural independence of wild offspring. John 8:36: “if the Son sets you free, you will be free indeed.” The freedom of the wild creature that grows up and departs is the type of the freedom that Christ brings: the children of God, set free by the Son, are not confined to the structures of the old creation’s bondage but go out into the freedom Christ won.
39.5: Who has let the wild donkey (onager) go free? Who has loosed the bonds of the swift donkey? — the untamed freedom of the wild ass. Rom 8:20–21: “for the creation was subjected to futility, not willingly, but because of him who subjected it, in hope that the creation itself will be set free from its bondage to corruption and obtain the freedom of the glory of the children of God.” The wild donkey’s freedom from human bondage is a foretaste of creation’s eschatological liberation that Christ’s redemption inaugurates.
39.6: YHWH making the wilderness the wild donkey’s home and the salt land its dwelling — the hostile desert as habitat, not prison. Luke 4:1: “Jesus, full of the Holy Spirit, returned from the Jordan and was led by the Spirit in the wilderness for forty days.” Christ in the wilderness does not flee the hostile environment but inhabits it as the new Adam who will not yield to the tempter. The salt-land as home for the wild free creature is the type of Christ dwelling in the desert of human suffering.
39.7: The wild donkey scorning the city’s tumult and not hearing the driver’s shout — radical independence from human control. John 18:36: “my kingdom is not of this world. If my kingdom were of this world, my servants would have been fighting.” Christ refuses the political kingdom offered by the tempter (Luke 4:5–8) and scorns the city’s attempt to make him king by force (John 6:15). The wild donkey’s independence from city-tumult is the type of Christ’s kingdom that operates by different logic than earthly power.
39.8: The wild donkey ranging the hills as pasture, searching for every green thing — the sovereign freedom of creaturely provision. Matt 6:26: “look at the birds of the air: they neither sow nor reap nor gather into barns, and yet your heavenly Father feeds them.” The wild donkey’s hill-ranging for food is the type of the Father’s provision for those who, like Christ, prioritize the kingdom and trust the Father for daily sustenance.
39.9: Will the wild ox serve you or spend the night at your manger? — the untameable re’em defying domestic service. Phil 2:7: “taking the form of a servant (doulos, a slave), being born in the likeness of men.” The wild ox that refuses the manger is the anti-type of Christ who voluntarily went to a manger (Luke 2:7) — not in servitude to human power but in the voluntary self-lowering of the Incarnation. The untameable God became tameable in the only way that matters: through love, not compulsion.
39.10: Can you bind the wild ox in the furrow or will it harrow the valleys after you? — the impossibility of harnessing untamed power for human work. Eph 1:19–20: “the immeasurable greatness of his power toward us who believe, according to the working of his great might that he worked in Christ when he raised him from the dead.” The power that cannot be bound in a furrow is the power that raised Christ from the dead and is now working in those who are in him.
39.11: Will you rely on the wild ox’s great strength and leave your labor to it? — the folly of depending on undomesticated power. John 15:5: “I am the vine; you are the branches. Whoever abides in me and I in him, he it is that bears much fruit, for apart from me you can do nothing.” The labor that cannot be left to the untameable wild ox can be entrusted to Christ, the Vine, who transmits his life to the branches and bears fruit through those connected to him.
39.12: Will the wild ox bring your grain home and gather it to your threshing floor? — the wild animal that will not harvest for the human economy. Matt 3:12: “his winnowing fork is in his hand, and he will clear his threshing floor and gather his wheat into the barn.” Christ, not a wild animal, is the one who gathers the harvest: the gathering to the heavenly barn is not entrusted to untameable creaturely force but to the Son who comes in judgment to gather his own.
39.13: The ostrich’s wing flapping joyfully — the wing of feathers without the stork’s wisdom or care. In the wisdom hierarchy of Job 38–39, the ostrich is notable as the creature from whom wisdom is specifically withheld (v17). In the NT, wisdom is specifically concentrated and given: 1 Cor 1:30 (“Christ Jesus, who became to us wisdom from God”); Col 2:3 (“in whom are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge”). The creature that lacks wisdom is the anti-type of Christ who is wisdom itself.
39.14: The ostrich leaving her eggs in the ground for the earth to warm them — the strange parenting inversion. Isa 49:15: “can a woman forget her nursing child, that she should have no compassion on the son of her womb? Even these may forget, yet I will not forget you.” The ostrich’s apparently careless maternal behavior highlights by contrast the divine maternal tenderness that reaches its fullest expression in Christ: “Jerusalem, Jerusalem…how often would I have gathered your children together as a hen gathers her brood under her wings” (Matt 23:37).
39.15: The ostrich forgetting that a foot may crush the eggs or a wild beast trample them — creaturely vulnerability without protective wisdom. John 10:28: “I give them eternal life, and they will never perish, and no one will snatch them out of my hand.” The eggs left unprotected on the ground are the anti-type of Christ’s sheep who are held in his hand and the Father’s hand — double protection that no foot can trample.
39.16: The ostrich dealing harshly with her young as though they were not hers — the maternal-inversion creature. Lam 4:3: “even jackals offer the breast; they nurse their young, but the daughter of my people has become cruel, like the ostriches in the wilderness.” The cruel city and the ostrich-mother are the backdrop for the tender father-figure of Christ: “see what kind of love the Father has given to us, that we should be called children of God” (1 John 3:1). The maternal rejection that the ostrich embodies is the opposite of the Incarnation’s embrace.
39.17: “God has made her forget wisdom and has not given her a share in understanding” — wisdom withheld from the ostrich by divine sovereign distribution. 1 Cor 1:27: “God chose what is foolish in the world to shame the wise.” The sovereign distribution of wisdom — withheld from the ostrich, poured out through the Spirit on the disciples at Pentecost (Acts 2:17) — is the divine prerogative. The wisdom denied to the ostrich is the wisdom given to the weak and foolish in Christ (1 Cor 2:12).
39.18: When the ostrich rouses herself and runs, she laughs at the horse and rider — the compensation of speed for lack of wisdom. 1 Cor 1:25: “the foolishness of God is wiser than men, and the weakness of God is stronger than horses.” The ostrich’s speed surpassing horse and rider is the type of the divine wisdom that appears foolish (the cross) but outruns every human wisdom-strategy. The laughing ostrich is the type of the God who holds the wise in derision (Ps 2:4) and whose apparent foolishness triumphs.
39.19: “Do you give the horse his might? Do you clothe his neck with a mane?” — YHWH as the source of the war horse’s power. Rev 19:11: “then I saw heaven opened, and behold, a white horse! The one sitting on it is called Faithful and True, and in righteousness he judges and makes war.” The war horse’s divine-given might is the animal type; the white horse of the parousia is the eschatological fulfillment. Christ is both the giver of the horse’s might and the rider of the heavenly horse at the final battle.
39.20: Making the war horse leap like the locust, its majestic snorting striking terror — the awesome power of the battle horse. Joel 2:4–5: “their appearance is like the appearance of horses, and like war horses they run…like the rumbling of chariots on the tops of mountains.” The locust-horse of Joel’s prophecy and the horses of Rev 9:7 are the eschatological escalation of Job 39’s war horse: the divine power displayed in creation is concentrated in the final eschatological battle where Christ leads the armies of heaven.
39.21: Pawing in the valley, exulting in strength, going out to meet weapons — the war horse’s fearless eagerness. Heb 12:2: “looking to Jesus, the founder and perfecter of our faith, who for the joy that was set before him endured the cross.” The war horse that goes out to meet weapons without fear is the type of Christ going to the cross “for the joy set before him” — not retreating from the weapons of suffering and death but meeting them directly.
39.22: Laughing at fear and not being dismayed, not turning back from the sword — the courage of the war horse. John 10:18: “no one takes it [my life] from me, but I lay it down of my own accord.” The war horse that does not flinch from the sword is the type of Christ who set his face toward Jerusalem (Luke 9:51) and went voluntarily to the death that would accomplish redemption. The fearlessness is not ignorance of danger but sovereign willingness to face it.
39.23: The quiver rattling against the horse, the spear and javelin flashing — the instruments of war that the courageous horse ignores. Isa 53:5: “he was pierced for our transgressions; he was crushed for our iniquities.” The spear that the war horse faces was literally planted in Christ’s side at Golgotha (John 19:34). The weapons that the horse faces bravely are the instruments of Christ’s passion; the war horse’s courage typifies the courage of the Servant who did not turn from the smiting (Isa 50:6).
39.24: Swallowing the ground with fierceness and rage, not standing still at the trumpet’s sound — the war horse’s total commitment at the charge. Rev 19:14–15: “the armies of heaven, arrayed in fine linen, white and pure, were following him on white horses. From his mouth comes a sharp sword with which to strike down the nations.” The war horse’s absolute commitment at the battle charge finds its fullest expression in Christ leading the armies of heaven in the final decisive battle against evil.
39.25: “Ha!” the horse says at the trumpet sound; smelling the battle from afar, the thunder of captains and the shouting — the war horse’s instinct for conflict. 1 Thess 4:16: “the Lord himself will descend from heaven with a cry of command, with the voice of an archangel, and with the sound of the trumpet of God.” The trumpet that sends the war horse charging is the type of the trumpet that heralds Christ’s parousia — the divine battle-call that sets in motion the final overthrow of all that opposes the kingdom.
39.26: “Is it by your understanding that the hawk soars and spreads his wings toward the south?” — the hawk’s migratory instinct beyond human teaching. Deut 32:11: “like an eagle that stirs up its nest, that flutters over its young, spreading out its wings, catching them, bearing them on its pinions.” God as eagle-parent is the OT background for Christ gathering Jerusalem’s children under his wings (Matt 23:37). The soaring hawk typifies the divine pastoral care that watches over and gathers the vulnerable.
39.27: “Is it at your command that the eagle mounts up and makes his nest on high?” — the eagle’s ascent beyond human command. Eph 4:10: “he who descended is the one who also ascended far above all the heavens, that he might fill all things.” The eagle ascending to make its nest on high is the type of Christ’s ascension: he who descended (the Incarnation) is the one who ascended “far above all the heavens.” The eagle’s high nest is the type of the session at the Father’s right hand.
39.28: The eagle dwelling on the rock, making its stronghold on the rocky crag — the impregnable height of the eagle’s refuge. Matt 16:18: “on this rock I will build my church, and the gates of hell shall not prevail against it.” The eagle’s rocky stronghold is the type of the church built on Christ the rock — equally impregnable from below. 1 Cor 10:4: “that rock was Christ” — the rock that the eagle inhabits is the rock that is Christ himself.
39.29: From the height the eagle spies out the prey, its eyes seeing from afar — the eagle’s panoramic vision. Heb 4:13: “no creature is hidden from God’s sight, but all are naked and exposed to the eyes of him to whom we must give account.” Rev 1:14: “his eyes were like a flame of fire” — the eyes of the risen Christ that see from afar. The eagle’s far-seeing eyes from the high crag are the type of Christ’s all-seeing gaze from the Father’s right hand.
39.30: The eagle’s young ones suck up blood, and where the slain are, there is the eagle — the eagle as gatherer at the place of death. Matt 24:28: “wherever the corpse is, there the vultures will gather.” This sign of judgment (the gathering of scavengers at the place of death) is Christ’s own sign for the parousia’s irreversible judgment. Rev 19:17–18: the angel calls the birds to the great supper of God where the dead of the final battle are consumed. The eagle at the slain is the sign of final divine judgment executed by Christ.

40.1: YHWH’s first challenge: “shall a faultfinder contend with the Almighty?” — the direct question that puts Job’s legal claim to the test. Rom 9:20: “who are you, O man, to answer back to God? Will what is molded say to its molder, ‘why have you made me like this?’” The challenge to the faultfinder is answered in the NT not by human silence alone but by Christ’s perfect advocacy: only the one who himself made no fault (1 Pet 2:22) could properly plead before God. Job’s silence (v4–5) anticipates the silence of all accusation before Christ’s perfect righteousness.
40.2: He who argues with God, let him answer — the demand for a real legal response. In the NT, the one who argues with God from a position of genuine righteousness is Christ himself: Heb 5:7 (“in the days of his flesh, Jesus offered up prayers and supplications, with loud cries and tears, to him who was able to save him from death”). Christ argues with God not from self-justification but from perfect prayer and submission; his cry of dereliction (Matt 27:46) is the deepest legal argument ever made before God, and the resurrection is God’s answer.
40.3: Job answers YHWH and says — the moment of the creature’s response before the Creator. This structural moment (creature speaking to Creator after the divine speeches) has its NT parallel in every prayer: the creature who has heard the Creator’s self-revelation in Christ now speaks in response. Eph 2:18: “through him [Christ] we both have access in one Spirit to the Father.” Job’s answer is the model of creaturely response: from within the overwhelming encounter, with diminished argument but with genuine address.
40.4: “Behold, I am of small account; what shall I answer you? I lay my hand on my mouth” — the silencing of the creature before the Creator. Phil 2:10–11: “at the name of Jesus every knee should bow, in heaven and on earth and under the earth, and every tongue confess that Jesus Christ is Lord.” The silenced mouth that lays its hand before God in Job 40:4 is the prototype of the universal confession before Christ at the parousia; the creature’s recognition of its smallness before the divine reaches its fullest expression in the acknowledgment of Christ’s lordship.
40.5: Once I have spoken and will not answer, twice and will add nothing more — the economy of the humbled creature’s speech. Matt 6:7: “when you pray, do not heap up empty phrases as the Gentiles do.” Luke 18:13: the tax collector who would not even lift his eyes but simply said “God, be merciful to me, a sinner.” Job’s silenced brevity is the model of genuine prayer: not the accumulation of legal arguments but the honest acknowledgment of creatureliness before God, which is itself an approach to the prayer that Christ teaches.
40.6: YHWH answering Job from the whirlwind a second time — the second divine speech. The double divine address from the storm is the structure of divine revelation: the first address is the question that humbles; the second address is the deeper revelation that heals. In the NT, the double speaking of the Father at Jesus’ baptism (Matt 3:17) and transfiguration (Matt 17:5) follows the same pattern of progressive divine revelation that discloses the Son more fully in the second address.
40.7: Gird up your loins like a man; I will question you and you make it known — the renewed call to courageous encounter. 1 Pet 1:13: “preparing your minds for action (lit. girding up the loins of your mind), being sober-minded, set your hope fully on the grace that will be brought to you at the revelation of Jesus Christ.” The loins-girding that YHWH demands of Job in the storm becomes, in the NT, the mental preparation for the parousia. The courageous encounter with God is now undertaken through hope in Christ’s revelation.
40.8: “Will you even put me in the wrong? Will you condemn me that you may be in the right?” — the sharpest question of the divine speeches. Rom 3:4: “let God be true though every one were a liar, as it is written, ‘that you may be justified in your words, and prevail when you are judged.’” Paul quotes Ps 51:4 here; the question that YHWH poses to Job — will you condemn God to justify yourself? — is answered by the gospel in the opposite direction: God justifies the sinner at the cost of condemning his own Son (Rom 8:3).
40.9: Do you have an arm like God’s, can you thunder with a voice like his? — the question of divine power. Rev 1:15: “his voice was like the roar of many waters.” The thundering divine voice of the whirlwind is the voice of the risen Christ in Revelation. The divine power (arm, voice, thunder) that only God possesses is the power that Christ shares with the Father (John 10:30: “I and the Father are one”; John 5:19: “whatever the Father does, the Son does likewise”).
40.10: Adorn yourself with majesty and dignity, clothe yourself with glory and splendor — the call to put on divine royal attributes. Phil 2:9–11: God has highly exalted Christ and given him the name above every name. The divine majesty, dignity, glory, and splendor that Job cannot clothe himself in are the very attributes that Christ shares from eternity and puts back on at his exaltation after the cross. 1 Tim 6:16: “who alone has immortality, who dwells in unapproachable light.”
40.11: Pour out the overflowings of your anger; look on everyone who is proud and abase him — the divine prerogative to judge the proud. Jas 4:6: “God opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble.” Luke 1:52: “he has brought down the mighty from their thrones and exalted those of humble estate.” The divine prerogative to abase the proud that Job cannot exercise is the prerogative that Christ’s cross and parousia exercise definitively: the cross humbles the proud by revealing that no human achievement merits justification; the parousia abases every earthly pride before the returning King.
40.12: Look on everyone who is proud and bring him low; tread down the wicked where they stand — the divine judgment that Job cannot execute. John 5:22: “the Father judges no one, but has given all judgment to the Son.” Acts 17:31: God “has fixed a day on which he will judge the world in righteousness by a man whom he has appointed.” The judgment of the proud and wicked that YHWH challenges Job to execute is the judgment that the Father has given to Christ: the Son will tread down the wicked at the appointed day.
40.13: Hide them all in the dust together; bind their faces in the world below — the disposal of the condemned. Rev 20:14–15: “then Death and Hades were thrown into the lake of fire. This is the second death.” The dust-binding and Sheol-binding that YHWH describes as his judicial prerogative is the prerogative that Christ exercises at the final judgment: the disposal of all that opposes the kingdom is the divine act committed to the Son (1 Cor 15:25: “he must reign until he has put all his enemies under his feet”).
40.14: “Then will I also praise you, for your own right hand can save you” — the ironic conditional: if Job can execute universal judgment, then God will acknowledge Job’s self-sufficiency. The right hand that saves in the OT is YHWH’s (Ps 98:1: “his right hand and his holy arm have worked salvation for him”); in the NT, the right hand is Christ’s position (Heb 1:3: “he sat down at the right hand of the Majesty on high”) after accomplishing the salvation that no human right hand could achieve.
40.15: “Look at Behemoth, which I made as I made you” — the creature solidarity of Behemoth and Job, both made by the same Creator. Phil 2:7: “taking the form of a servant, being born in the likeness of men.” The “made as I made you” — creaturely solidarity between the powerful and the vulnerable — is the OT type of the Incarnation: the eternal Son took on the same creaturely status (“likeness of men”) as those he came to redeem. The Creator becoming a creature is the supreme expression of the solidarity that v15 implies.
40.16: His strength is in his loins, his power in the muscles of his belly — Behemoth’s physical power concentrated in the body’s core. Eph 6:10: “be strong in the Lord and in the strength of his might.” The strength of Behemoth that no human warrior can match is the strength that Christ makes available to those in him — not through physical might but through the Spirit: “strengthened with power through his Spirit in your inner being” (Eph 3:16). The interior power that Behemoth displays externally, Christ supplies inwardly to the believer.
40.17: His tail moves like a cedar, the sinews of his thighs are knit together — the extraordinary strength of Behemoth’s body structure. 1 Cor 15:44: “it is sown a natural body; it is raised a spiritual body.” The resurrection body that Christ was first to receive (v20: “the firstfruits”) is the perfection of creaturely embodiment: the glorified body that Behemoth’s extraordinary physicality faintly typifies. Christ’s resurrection body is the first example of the new creation’s embodied power (Phil 3:21: “he will transform our lowly body to be like his glorious body”).
40.18: His bones are tubes of bronze, his limbs like bars of iron — the impenetrable body of Behemoth. Acts 2:24: “God raised him up, loosing the pangs of death, because it was not possible for him to be held by it.” The bronze-bone, iron-limbed Behemoth that nothing can pierce is the creaturely type of Christ’s resurrection body that death could not hold. The “pangs of death” that could not contain Christ are the force that Behemoth’s impenetrable frame defies at a creaturely level.
40.19: “He is the first of the works of God; let him who made him bring near his sword” — Behemoth as the chief of the works of creation, challengeable only by his Maker. Col 1:15: “he is the image of the invisible God, the firstborn of all creation.” Behemoth as “first of the works of God” (re’shit) is the creaturely type of Christ as “firstborn of all creation” (prototokos) — not the first creature made but the one who holds primacy over all creation. Only the Maker can bring the sword to Behemoth; only the cross could accomplish what creation could not.
40.20: The mountains yield their produce for him; all the wild beasts play where he lies — Behemoth as the center of the natural order. John 1:3: “all things were made through him, and without him was not any thing made that was made.” The Behemoth that the natural world serves and gathers around is the creaturely type of Christ as the one through whom all things exist and toward whom all things ultimately move (Rom 11:36: “from him and through him and to him are all things”).
40.21: Under the lotus plants he lies, in the shelter of the reeds and in the marsh — Behemoth resting in the wetlands. John 1:14: “the Word became flesh and dwelt (eskenosen, tented/tabernacled) among us.” The great creature resting in the marsh’s shelter is the type of the Son taking his place in creaturely habitat. Christ who “tabernacled” among humanity rested in the specific ecological conditions of the Jordan valley and the reed-edged Sea of Galilee — the same terrain Behemoth inhabits.
40.22: The lotus trees cover him for shade; the willows of the brook surround him — the natural canopy over Behemoth. Ps 91:4: “he will cover you with his pinions, and under his wings you will find refuge.” The shade-giving lotus over Behemoth is the type of the divine covering over those who take refuge in God. Matt 23:37: the hen covering chicks under wings is Christ’s own image for his protection of Jerusalem. The Behemoth shaded in the marsh becomes, by inversion, the one who provides shade and protection for others.
40.23: If the river is turbulent he is not frightened; he is confident though Jordan rushes against his mouth — Behemoth’s confidence in the Jordan’s rushing flood. Matt 3:13–17: Christ’s baptism in the Jordan River. The Jordan that Behemoth is not frightened by is the Jordan in which Christ was baptized — identifying with sinners under judgment and emerging to the Father’s declaration: “this is my beloved Son, with whom I am well pleased.” The confident creature in the Jordan typifies Christ’s confident submission to baptismal waters of judgment.
40.24: Can one take him by his eyes or pierce his nose with a snare? — the unhuntable Behemoth. Luke 11:22: the “stronger man” who comes upon the strong man and overcomes him takes his armor and divides the spoil. Behemoth cannot be caught; but the “stronger man” of Christ’s parable points to the one who can overcome the unhuntable — by going through death and resurrection rather than by conventional conquest. What no human snare can capture, the cross and resurrection accomplished.

41.1: “Can you draw out Leviathan with a fishhook?” — the opening challenge of the Leviathan speech. Matt 12:29: “how can someone enter a strong man’s house and plunder his goods, unless he first binds the strong man?” Leviathan, the primordial chaos-serpent, is the “strong man” that no human fisher can hook. Christ is the only one who can draw out and bind Leviathan — not through superior creaturely force but through the victory of the cross (Col 2:15: “disarming the rulers and authorities and putting them to open shame, triumphing over them in him”).
41.2: Can you put a rope in his nose or pierce his jaw with a hook? — the impossibility of controlling the sea-monster. Isa 27:1: “in that day the LORD with his hard and great and strong sword will punish Leviathan the fleeing serpent, Leviathan the twisting serpent, and he will slay the dragon that is in the sea.” The hook that no creature can drive through Leviathan’s jaw is wielded by YHWH in the eschatological day; in the NT this is identified with Christ’s final victory: Rev 20:2 (“he seized the dragon, that ancient serpent, who is the devil and Satan, and bound him”).
41.3: Will Leviathan speak with soft words to you, will he make a covenant with you? — the impossibility of diplomacy with the chaos-monster. Heb 2:14: “since therefore the children share in flesh and blood, he himself likewise partook of the same things, that through death he might destroy the one who has the power of death, that is, the devil.” Leviathan does not make covenants on human terms; Christ did not negotiate with death but destroyed it by undergoing it. The new covenant that Christ makes is not a diplomatic arrangement with the serpent but the serpent’s defeat.
41.4: Will he make a covenant with you to be taken as a servant forever? — the impossibility of taming Leviathan for perpetual service. Rev 20:3: Satan “bound…so that he might not deceive the nations any longer, until the thousand years were ended.” The permanent taming of Leviathan that no human can accomplish is the accomplished fact of Christ’s victory: the ancient serpent will be not only bound but cast into the lake of fire (Rev 20:10), permanently removed from his role as deceiver.
41.5: Will you play with Leviathan as with a bird, or put him on a leash for your girls? — the absurdity of treating the chaos-monster as a pet. Col 2:15: “he disarmed the rulers and authorities and put them to open shame, by triumphing over them in him.” The enemies of creation that could never be made into pets are put to open shame by the cross: their power is publicly displayed as defeated. What appears to be Leviathan’s triumph at the cross is the place where Christ “put them to open shame.”
41.6: Will traders make a bargain for him, will they divide him up among the merchants? — the economic impossibility of commercializing the chaos-monster. 1 Pet 1:18–19: “you were ransomed from the futile ways inherited from your forefathers, not with perishable things such as silver or gold, but with the precious blood of Christ, like that of a lamb without blemish or spot.” No merchant can bargain for Leviathan; but Christ paid the ransom that no commercial transaction could accomplish — not silver or gold but his own blood.
41.7: Can you fill his hide with harpoons or his head with fish spears? — the piercing of the impenetrable hide. Zech 12:10: “they will look on me, on him whom they have pierced.” John 19:34: “one of the soldiers pierced his side with a spear, and at once blood and water came out.” The impenetrable Leviathan whom no spear can pierce is the anti-type of Christ whose side was pierced — the one who was vulnerable to the very weapon that cannot reach the chaos-serpent. Christ absorbed the wound that Leviathan is immune to.
41.8: “Lay your hands on him; remember the battle; you will not do it again!” — the fearful warning against trying to fight Leviathan. Heb 2:14–15: “that through death he might destroy the one who has the power of death…and deliver all those who through fear of death were subject to lifelong slavery.” Those enslaved by the fear of death-in-battle with Leviathan — never daring to lay hands on him — are set free by Christ who fought and destroyed death on their behalf. The warning of v8 is the freedom of those liberated by Christ’s vicarious battle.
41.9: Behold, any hope of overcoming Leviathan is false; at the sight of him one is cast down — the hopelessness of the creature before the chaos-monster. Rom 7:24: “wretched man that I am! Who will deliver me from this body of death?” The hopelessness that any human faces against Leviathan is the spiritual despair of the one who cannot defeat the power of sin and death through self-effort. The answer: v25: “thanks be to God through Jesus Christ our Lord!” — the deliverer who overcomes where all human hope is false.
41.10: No one is so fierce that he would dare to stir up Leviathan — the universal creaturely fear. Rev 5:3–5: “no one in heaven or on earth or under the earth was able to open the scroll…the Lion of the tribe of Judah, the Root of David, has conquered.” The scroll that no creature can open and the Leviathan that no creature dares stir are the same problem: the deficiency of all creaturely power before the ultimate challenge. The Lamb who takes the scroll is the Christ who alone dared to stir Leviathan — and conquered.
41.11: “Who has first given to me, that I should repay him? Whatever is under the whole heaven is mine” — the divine ownership of all things, pre-empting any human claim on God. Rom 11:35–36: “or who has given a gift to him that he might be repaid? For from him and through him and to him are all things.” Paul quotes this very logic in his doxology concluding the argument for divine sovereignty in salvation. The divine ownership that YHWH asserts over Leviathan is the ownership that Christ shares: John 17:10: “all mine are yours, and yours are mine.”
41.12: “I will not keep silence concerning his limbs, or his mighty strength, or his goodly frame” — YHWH speaking fully about Leviathan’s design.
